entry.title: Stop Users from Typing Invalid Values in SAPUI5 ComboBox – A Practical Validation Approach
entry.content:
Introduction

In one of my recent SAPUI5 projects, I needed users to select a value from a predefined list. Since the list was quite large, I wanted to make the selection process as smooth as possible. Instead of using sap.m.Select, I went with sap.m.ComboBox because it lets users search for values by typing, making it much easier to find the right option.

Everything worked as expected until I started writing unit test cases. While testing different scenarios, I noticed that users weren't limited to selecting values from the dropdown they could also type any value they wanted. Even if the entered text didn't exist in the list, the ComboBox would still accept it.

It might seem like obvious behavior once you know how ComboBox works, but it was something I hadn't considered during development. That's when I realized that while the control offers a great user experience, it doesn't automatically guarantee valid input.

We could have used a value help dialog for selection, but the requirement specifically called for a dropdown-based experience. That’s why the focus stayed on ComboBox  and a deeper validation approach around it.

In this article, I'll walk through the approach I used to keep the search functionality intact while ensuring users can only submit values that actually exist in the dropdown.

 

The Real Issue in Applications

In actual business scenarios, this becomes a real problem when data moves to the backend. Users may enter free text in the ComboBox and it still gets treated as valid input even if it doesn’t exist in the master data.

This can lead to invalid payloads, failed backend validations, and inconsistent business data in reports. What starts as a small UI behavior can quickly turn into a data integrity issue.

That’s why, when using sap.m.ComboBox for strictly controlled fields like Country, Plant, or Company Code, frontend validation becomes essential before processing the input.

 Picture 1: Understanding the ComboBox and It's Behaviour

 

The Approach I Used

To handle this, I added a simple validation on the ComboBox events. The idea was straightforward—accept the input only if it matches a valid item from the list.

I mainly relied on the change event along with getSelectedKey(). If the user entered something that didn’t match any item, I cleared the value, showed an error state using setValueState("Error"), and displayed a meaningful message.

I also used selectionChange to reset the error state when a valid option was selected from the dropdown, ensuring a smoother user experience.

 

Implementation

The validation was handled mainly using the change and selectionChange events. The goal was to ensure that only valid values from the dropdown are accepted, while giving clear feedback when something incorrect is entered.

XML View Code: (.xml file)
<!-- XML View -->
<ComboBox
    id="countryCombo"
    items="{path: '/Countries'}"
    selectionChange=".onSelectionChange"
    change=".onChange">
    <core:Item
        key="{CountryCode}"
        text="{CountryName}" />
</ComboBox>
Controller Code: (.js file)
// Controller Logic
onChange: function (oEvent) {
    const oCombo = oEvent.getSource();
    const sValue = oCombo.getValue().trim();
    const sKey = oCombo.getSelectedKey();
    if (!sValue || !sKey) {
        oCombo.setValue("");
        oCombo.setValueState("Error");
        oCombo.setValueStateText("Please select a valid value from the list.");
        return;
    }
    oCombo.setValueState("None");
},

onSelectionChange: function (oEvent) {
    oEvent.getSource().setValueState("None");
}

 

Best Practices

When working with sap.m.ComboBox, validation should always be treated as part of the design, not an afterthought.

Use getSelectedKey() instead of relying only on getValue(), since free text input is always possible. Trim user input before validation to avoid whitespace-related issues.

Always provide clear ValueState feedback so users immediately understand what went wrong. Also, reset the error state on valid selection using selectionChange to improve usability.

Finally, even if frontend validation is in place, always enforce the same checks at the backend to ensure data integrity.

 Picture 2: Data Integrity

 

Alternative Approaches

Depending on the requirement, there are a few other ways to handle this.

If search is not critical and the list is small, sap.m.Select is a simpler option since it only allows predefined selections.

In some cases, developers try disabling free typing in ComboBox, but that removes the search flexibility, which is often the main reason for choosing it in the first place.

Another option is model or backend-level validation, but that should always complement frontend checks rather than replace them.

  Picture 3: Right Approaches for Handling Selections

 

Key Takeaway & Conclusion:

A ComboBox is not automatically restricted to its items. If your requirement is to allow only predefined values, you must explicitly validate the user input before processing it.

The goal is not to remove the flexibility of sap.m.ComboBox, but to balance it—keep the search experience intact while ensuring only valid, business-approved values make it to the backend.

Kindly Note: We could have used a value help dialog for selection, but the requirement specifically called for a dropdown-based experience. That’s why the focus stayed on sap.m.ComboBox and a deeper validation approach around it.

If you have any alternative effective solutions, please feel free to share your thoughts and so everyone can learn from each other.

Thank you for taking the time to read this blog! If you found this helpful, i would love to hear your thoughts, feedback or questions in the comments. Let's keep learning and growing together!

I’m still new to blogging, so if you notice anything that could be improved or corrected, please don’t hesitate to let me know!!

entry.title: Deploy a SAP Fiori Elements App from BAS to S/4HANA Cloud and Register It as a Launchpad Tile
entry.content:
Most tutorials about deploying Fiori apps from Business Application Studio (BAS) stop at 'Deployment Successful.' But deployment is only the midpoint. For a user to actually find and launch your app in the SAP Fiori Launchpad, you need to wire up four more things: a crossNavigation inbound in the manifest, an IAM App, a Business Catalog, and a Launchpad Space. Get any one of these wrong and you hit silent failures — the app exists in the system but never shows as a tile.

Prerequisites

  • SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) subaccount with SAP Business Application Studio subscription
  • S/4HANA Cloud system with an active OData V4 service
  • A BTP Destination configured for your S/4HANA system
  • Eclipse ADT connected to your S/4HANA system

Architecture Overview

OData V4 Service (S/4HANA ABAP)
        │
        ▼
Fiori Elements List Report App  ◄── Generated in SAP BAS
        │
   npm run deploy  (deploy-to-abap, ui5-deploy.yaml, gCTS transport)
        │
        ▼
BSP Application + LADI /NAMESPACE/MYAPP_UI5R  (auto-created on deploy)
        │
        ▼
IAM App (External App type)  ◄── Created in ADT, Published Locally
        │
        ▼
Business Catalog  ◄── Created in ADT, Published Locally
        │
        ▼
Business Role  ◄── Assigned in Maintain Business Roles
        │
        ▼
Launchpad Space / Page  ◄── Tile visible to user

 

 

Step 1 — Open Business Application Studio via BTP Cockpit

BAS is accessed through the SAP BTP Cockpit — not through the S/4HANA Fiori Launchpad. Open BTP Cockpit → Subaccount → Instances and Subscriptions.

Find SAP Business Application Studio → click Go to Application. You land in the SAP Build Lobby.

Step 2 — Create the Project in SAP Build

In the SAP Build Lobby, click Create (top right) to launch the Create Project wizard.

Wizard Step 1 — Objective: Application

Wizard Step 2 — Category: Full-Stack

Wizard Step 3 — Type: Full-Stack Node.JS

Wizard Step 4 — Name and Dev Space

Provide a project name (e.g. myapp-audit-log) and create or select a Full-Stack dev space.

Click Review then Create. SAP Build provisions the dev space and scaffolds the project.

Step 3 — Generate the Fiori App

With the project open in BAS, press Ctrl+Shift+P and run: Fiori: Open Application Generator

Template Selection — List Report Page

Data Source — Connect to a System

  • Data Source: Connect to a System
  • System: Your S/4HANA destination (e.g. YOUR_SYSTEM (S4HC))

Once connected, choose your OData V4 service.

Entity Selection

  • Main Entity: Root CDS entity exposed by the service
  • Automatically add table columns: Yes
  • Table Type: Responsive

Click Finish. BAS generates and opens the project.

Step 4 — Add the crossNavigation Inbound to manifest.json

⚠️  This is where most guides go wrong by omission.

By default the Fiori generator creates an application-type app with no crossNavigation block. If you deploy as-is, the BAS terminal shows:

warn  No LADI was created as no inbound is defined in manifest.json
warn  App descriptor of type 'application' does not define a launchable inbound

Without a Launchpad App Descriptor Item (LADI), you cannot create an IAM App that references your app, and therefore cannot add a tile to a Business Catalog.

Fix: Open webapp/manifest.json and add a crossNavigation block inside sap.app:

"sap.app": {
  "id": "com.mycompany.myauditlog",
  "type": "application",
  "crossNavigation": {
    "inbounds": {
      "MyAuditLog-display": {
        "semanticObject": "MyAuditLog",
        "action": "display",
        "title": "{{flpTitle}}",
        "subTitle": "{{flpSubtitle}}",
        "signature": {
          "parameters": {},
          "additionalParameters": "allowed"
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

Also add the keys to webapp/i18n/i18n.properties:

flpTitle=My Audit Log App
flpSubtitle=View sales order change history

 

📝  Tip on additionalParameters: Setting it to "" produces a harmless warning. Use the explicit string "allowed" or "ignored" to avoid it.

 

 

Step 5 — Create a gCTS Transport Request in ADT

The deploy-to-abap task requires a gCTS transport — a Workbench Request with target 1GT. A normal transport fails with:

Request does not have transport target 1GT

In ADT: Transport Organizer → right-click your user node → Create → Transport Request:

  • Type: Workbench Request
  • Target: 1GT

Note the transport number (e.g. ABC9xxxxx) — you will plug it into ui5-deploy.yaml.

Step 6 — Configure ui5-deploy.yaml

Create ui5-deploy.yaml in the project root:

specVersion: "4.0"
metadata:
  name: com.mycompany.myauditlog
type: application
builder:
  resources:
    excludes:
      - /test/**
      - /localService/**
  customTasks:
    - name: deploy-to-abap
      afterTask: generateCachebusterInfo
      configuration:
        target:
          destination: YOUR_SYSTEM
          authenticationType: reentranceTicket
        app:
          name: /MYNAMESPACE/MYAUDITLOG
          description: My Audit Log App
          package: /MYNAMESPACE/MY_PACKAGE
          transport: ABCK9xxxxx
        exclude:
          - /test/
          - /localService/

⚠️  app.name must use your registered ABAP namespace prefix (e.g. /MYNAMESPACE/). Using a Z* name in a namespaced package causes: Customer object WAPA cannot be assigned to package

Step 7 — Build and Deploy

Open a terminal in the project root and run:

npm install
npm run build
npm run deploy

A successful deployment shows:

Inbound MyAuditLog-display was converted to LADI /MYNAMESPACE/MYAUDITLOG_UI5R
Launchpad App Descriptor Item /MYNAMESPACE/MYAUDITLOG_UI5R was created
SAPUI5 Application has been uploaded and registered successfully
Deployment Successful.

📝  Note the LADI ID (/MYNAMESPACE/MYAUDITLOG_UI5R) — you need it for the IAM App in the next step.

Step 8 — Create the IAM App in ADT

In ADT: right-click your package → New → Other ABAP Repository Object → search for IAM App.

Fill in:

  • Name: /MYNAMESPACE/MYAUDITLOG_APP
  • Description: My Audit Log Fiori App
  • App Type: External App  ← critical (see gotcha below)
  • Fiori Launchpad App Descr Item ID: /MYNAMESPACE/MYAUDITLOG_UI5R

Select your transport request and click Finish.

Click Publish Locally.

⚠️  Do NOT select 'Fiori App' or 'UI Adaptation App' as the type. The UI5R descriptor created by deploy-to-abap is only compatible with External App. Wrong type gives:
    UI5R can only be used in Adaptation UI Apps

📝  Publishing auto-generates _UI5A and _UI5_EXT runtime artifacts. Always reference the IAM App by its logical name /MYNAMESPACE/MYAUDITLOG_APP, not the runtime objects. 

Step 9 — Create and Publish the Business Catalog

In ADT: right-click your package → New → Other ABAP Repository Object → Business Catalog.

Name it (e.g. /MYNAMESPACE/MYAUDITLOG_BC), select your transport, click Finish.

In the Apps tab, click Add and search for your logical IAM App name.

Save, then click Publish Locally.

⚠️  The catalog MUST be Published before it appears in:
  • The Maintain Business Roles app's Business Catalog picker
  • The Select Catalogs dialog in the Launchpad page editor
If you can see a colleague's catalog but not yours, come back and verify the Publication State.

Step 10 — Assign the Catalog to a Business Role

In the Fiori Launchpad, open the Maintain Business Roles app:

  • Open your role → Edit
  • Business Catalogs tab → Add → search for /MYNAMESPACE/MYAUDITLOG_BC → OK
  • Set Access Category to Unrestricted
  • Save

⚠️  The Select Catalogs dialog in the page editor (next step) only lists catalogs already assigned to the role that owns the space. Skip this step and your catalog won't appear in the dialog even if it's published. 

Step 11 — Add the Tile to a Launchpad Space

In Maintain Business Roles → your role → Launchpad Spaces tab:

  1. Click Add → Create New Space

  1. Open the space/page → Edit → click Select Catalogs

  1. Tick your catalog → Select
  2. On the Manually Selected tab, drag the tile into the page
  3. Add a Section Title, then Save

Step 12 — Access the App

Hard-refresh the Fiori Launchpad (Ctrl+Shift+R). Navigate to your space.

Click the tile. The List Report loads. Press Go.

Troubleshooting Cheat Sheet

Symptom

Root Cause

Fix

warn No LADI was created

No crossNavigation.inbounds in manifest.json

Add the crossNavigation block before deploying

Request does not have transport target 1GT

Wrong transport type

Create a gCTS transport (target 1GT) in ADT

Customer object WAPA cannot be assigned to package

Z* app name in a namespaced package

Use namespaced app name: /MYNAMESPACE/MYAPP

UI5R can only be used in Adaptation UI Apps

Wrong IAM App type

Create IAM App with type External App

My catalog missing from Select Catalogs

Not published or not assigned to the role

Publish in ADT + assign to Business Role + reload

403 Forbidden on BSP URL

UCON blocks direct access

Access via the Launchpad tile, not the direct URL

 

Summary

The key insight this guide captures is that deployment and launchpad registration are separate concerns that must be completed correctly and in order:

  1. crossNavigation inbound → enables LADI creation on deploy
  2. gCTS transport → enables deploy-to-abap
  3. IAM App (External App) → links the LADI to the authorization layer
  4. Business Catalog (Published) → groups the app for role assignment
  5. Business Role (catalog assigned) → makes the catalog visible in the page editor
  6. Launchpad page (tile dragged in) → makes the tile visible to the user

 Tested on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP Build / BAS (2024/2025 release), @SAP-ux/ui5-deploy task via specVersion: '4.0' ui5-deploy.yaml.

entry.title: Your Voice on the Future of SAP UI Technology
entry.content:
AI is reshaping how enterprise applications are built, used, and experienced. This raises fundamental questions for SAP's UI technology strategy - and we want to hear from you.

TL;DR: We are running a short, anonymous survey on the future of enterprise UI development with SAP. It takes 5 minutes and is open from July 1 to July 19, 2026. Take the survey →

What are we asking?

We have put together a short survey on the future of enterprise UI development with SAP. It covers topics such as:

  • How well SAPUI5 meets your needs today - and in the age of AI
  • Which UI experiences you expect to dominate enterprise apps in the coming years
  • Which capabilities matter most for your future development work
  • Where you see SAP's UI technology strategy heading

Why your input matters

SAP's UI frameworks power thousands of enterprise applications worldwide. Decisions about their future direction affect you — whether you are building new apps, maintaining existing ones, or advising customers on their architecture. Your perspective as a developer, architect, or decision maker in the SAP ecosystem is what we need to make the right calls.

Who should take this survey?

This survey is for anyone working with SAP UI technologies: SAPUI5 / Fiori developers, UI and UX architects, technical leads, enterprise architects, and IT decision makers at SAP customers and partners.

Call to Action

Make your voice heard by participating in the survey here: SAP UI Technology Strategy Survey. It only takes 5 minutes to complete, and is open from July 1 to July 19, 2026.

entry.title: Project HomeControl 2 · Part 1 — 10 years later, my whole house talks (and an AI drew the dashboard)
entry.content:
Sequel to Project HomeControl (2016). A short series about wiring up a modern smart home — and, running underneath it, what you can actually do with AI today. Every part ends with one concrete lesson on building with an agent. All passwords, tokens and keys are deliberately left out.

Ten years ago I soldered my meter cupboard to UI5

In March 2016 I wrote my first Project HomeControl post. The trigger: the EU was rolling out smart meters, the Netherlands would replace every old meter before 2020, and I had an electric car and wanted to know what it did to my consumption.

The solution back then was gloriously hacky: an Arduino on a breadboard, a wire in the P1 port of the meter, reading serial number, tariff, current and total usage — shown in a UI5 app I built myself.

Ten years on, almost everything about that story has changed, and one thing hasn't.

The thing I soldered? It's now a HomeWizard P1 — a €30 plug that does exactly the same, reliably, out of the box. The electric car is a Porsche Taycan. And it's no longer one meter: it's a whole house — solar, a heat pump, chargers, Hue, Sonos, thirteen cameras, blinds.

What didn't change: the curiosity, and — spoiler for Part 2 — that it ends up being about UI5 again.

This is Project HomeControl 2. This part is the boring-but-important half: the setup, and a first dashboard. The fun half (an AI agent that did most of the work) is Part 3.

What's in the house now

Everything reports into one hub — Home Assistant, running in Docker on a QNAP NAS. The integrations fall into three neat buckets, and that split matters later:

  • Local, real-time — SolarEdge inverter over Modbus TCP, the HomeWizard P1 meter, the Tesla Wall Connector's local API, the heat pump via a CMI gateway.
  • Local, discovered — Philips Hue (79 lamps, 83 scenes), Sonos, the Brel blinds hub.
  • Cloud — Porsche Connect for the Taycan, Ring for the cameras.

By the end of the setup, Home Assistant held 424 entities across eight integrations. The 2016 version tracked about six values from one meter. Progress.

Three kinds of integration feed one hub: local and real-time where it matters (solar, meter, charger), local discovery for lighting and media, cloud where there's no other way in. Home Assistant is the single source of truth the dashboard reads from.

Design before framework: a static dashboard first

Here's a habit worth stealing: before reaching for a framework, I build the dashboard as a single static HTML file. No build step, no components, no bindings — just a design I can look at and react to. It's the cheapest possible way to decide what the thing should feel like before committing to how it's built.

The result is a small, deliberate design system:

  • Design tokens for colour, surfaces and text, with a genuine dark and light theme (not a naive invert — each theme tuned so the accent still reads).
  • Tabular numerals everywhere digits line up, so values don't jitter as they update.
  • Status encoded in form, not just text — a green "Live" chip, an amber "Almost done", a red battery figure — so what needs attention reads at a glance.
  • A live energy-flow: where the power is going right now, and how much of it the sun covers.

The static dashboard (dark theme). Summary tiles up top, then energy, car & charging, and the house — every number is real data from Home Assistant. There's a light theme too, driven by the same design tokens, so one set of components serves both.

That energy-flow card tells a true story from one afternoon: the Taycan was charging at 11 kW while the sun only produced 3.1 kW, so 8.9 kW came from the grid. Seeing that immediately raised the question that becomes Part 4 — can I make the car charge on sunshine only?

The part that surprised me

I didn't hand-code that dashboard pixel by pixel. I described it — "a clean, dark energy dashboard, tokenised, dark and light, tabular numbers, status chips" — and an AI agent produced the design system, wired it to the live Home Assistant data, and rendered it in both themes. My job shifted from typing CSS to directing taste: react, refine, decide.

That's the thread running through this whole series. Not "AI writes code" — we knew that — but that the unit of work moves up a level. You bring the intent and the judgement; the agent brings the hours.

What this shows about building with AI

A plain-language description — "make it feel like a clean energy dashboard" — became a real, themeable design system wired to live data, in minutes. The lesson isn't speed for its own sake; it's that the cheap-to-throw-away exploration phase gets almost free. You can see three directions before lunch and keep the one with taste. Start vaguer than you think, then direct.

Next

The dashboard is static HTML today. In Part 2, I turn it into a proper UI5 frontend — and the interesting bit is that I do it with non-UI5 Web Components, hosted inside a UI5 app. It's the same question I asked in 2016, only reversed, with a much better answer.

Part 2 — A UI5 frontend with (non-UI5) Web Components — is next.
https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/project-homecontrol-2-part-2-a-ui5-frontend-built-from-non-ui5-web/ba-p/14433981

entry.title: Project HomeControl 2 · Part 2 — A UI5 frontend, built from non-UI5 Web Components
entry.content:
Part 2 of Project HomeControl 2. In Part 1 I connected the whole house to Home Assistant and drew a dashboard as static HTML. Now I make it a real UI5 frontend — using Web Components that aren't UI5. As with every part, there's one lesson about building with an AI agent at the end.

The question I couldn't let go

In 2016 I built a UI5 app to show my smart meter. In Part 1 of this sequel I had a dashboard again — but as a static HTML file with a bespoke design system: dark tokens, tabular numbers, status chips, a live energy-flow. Lovely to look at, but not a UI5 app.

Rebuilding that look in stock Fiori controls would mean fighting the theme every step of the way. So I asked the question a lot of UI5 developers quietly wonder about:

Can I keep this exact design — as Web Components — and still host it in a UI5 app, with proper model binding, in an XML view, driven by a JSONModel?

Yes. Here is the whole thing, end to end, including the parts that bit me.

Why UI5, and not React or Astro?

Fair question — and an honest answer: if I were building this dashboard greenfield, I probably wouldn't reach for UI5. For a bespoke, consumer-style dashboard, Astro, React or Lit are the natural picks. UI5 is built for enterprise Fiori apps with OData, not dark energy dashboards.

But that's not the interesting scenario. The interesting one is the reverse:

Nobody starts a bespoke dashboard in UI5. Plenty of teams have a large UI5/Fiori landscape and want to bring modern, shared Web Components into it — without rewriting.

That's a real, non-niche need: reuse a design-system component across apps, drop in a third-party Web Component (a charting library, a Shoelace control), or share slices of UI between UI5 and non-UI5 apps as micro-frontends. In that world, the dashboard here is just the vehicle — the point is that your UI5 investment can host framework-agnostic components, with binding.

And it's oddly under-documented. SAP even ships "UI5 Web Components" — but that's the other direction (UI5 controls exposed as custom elements for React/Angular). Hosting arbitrary Web Components inside a classic UI5 app, with model binding and the sharp edges, is territory most UI5 content skips. That's exactly why it's worth writing down.

A 30-second recap: what a Web Component is

A Web Component is three browser standards working together:

class PeppieTile extends HTMLElement {       // 1. a custom element
  constructor() { super(); this.attachShadow({ mode: "open" }); }   // 2. Shadow DOM (scoped CSS)
  set data(d) {                              // 3. a PROPERTY that takes an object
    this.shadowRoot.innerHTML = `<style>…</style><div class="tile">${d.value} ${d.unit}</div>`;
  }
}
customElements.define("peppie-tile", PeppieTile);   // tag MUST contain a hyphen

Use it with plain DOM:

const t = document.createElement("peppie-tile");
t.data = { label: "Solar now", value: "3.1", unit: "kW", accent: "amber" };
document.body.append(t);

No framework. It runs standalone, in React, and — the point of this post — in UI5.

The bridge: one UI5 control

Am I not reinventing something SAP already ships? Partly — yes, and it's worth saying up front. UI5 has an official base class, sap.ui.core.webc.WebComponent, that does this generically: it renders the tag, forwards object values onto the element as properties (not attributes), updates them without a full re-render, and maps slots and events. For a fixed set of components, extending it per element is the idiomatic route — and a lot less code than the wrapper below. Nico Schönteich walks through exactly that in Consuming your own (or external) Web Components in UI5 Applications. I'm rolling the glue by hand here on purpose: it's the clearest way to show why .data has to be a property, and where the upgrade-order and re-render traps live. Do it once yourself, then reach for the base class. (I also wanted one generic control that wraps any tag via a single data object — a different trade-off, handy for a fast-moving bespoke design system.)

UI5 already renders DOM. A custom element is DOM. So the bridge is a single control that renders the tag and hands the element its data:

// peppie/control/WebComponent.js
sap.ui.define(["sap/ui/core/Control"], function (Control) {
  "use strict";
  return Control.extend("peppie.control.WebComponent", {
    metadata: {
      properties: {
        tag:  { type: "string", defaultValue: "div" },  // which custom element
        data: { type: "object" }                        // bind your model object here
      }
    },
    renderer: {
      apiVersion: 2,                                     // semantic rendering / DOM patching
      render: function (rm, oControl) {
        rm.openStart(oControl.getTag(), oControl);       // <peppie-tile id="__control0">
        rm.style("display", "block");
        rm.openEnd();
        rm.close(oControl.getTag());
      }
    },
    onAfterRendering: function () {
      var oDom = this.getDomRef();
      if (oDom) { oDom.data = this.getData(); }          // property, not attribute (pitfall #1)
    },
    setData: function (vData) {
      this.setProperty("data", vData, true /* suppress re-render */);
      var oDom = this.getDomRef();
      if (oDom) { oDom.data = vData; }                   // live update in place (pitfall #3)
      return this;
    }
  });
});

rm.openStart(tag, oControl) is the trick in the renderer: passing the control writes UI5's id and bookkeeping onto the custom element, so apiVersion: 2 can patch it in place instead of throwing it away on every change.

Wiring it into a real UI5 app

Four small pieces, all of them standard UI5.

1. Bootstrap — load the custom elements before UI5, register a resource root for your control namespace, and pick a theme:

<!-- index.html -->
<script src="webcomponents.js"></script>            <!-- define elements first (pitfall #4) -->
<script id="sap-ui-bootstrap"
  src="https://sdk.openui5.org/resources/sap-ui-core.js"
  data-sap-ui-theme="sap_horizon_dark"
  data-sap-ui-libs="sap.m,sap.ui.layout"
  data-sap-ui-resourceroots='{"peppie": "./peppie/"}'
  data-sap-ui-oninit="module:sap/ui/core/ComponentSupport"
  data-sap-ui-compatVersion="edge"
  data-sap-ui-async="true"></script>

2. The model — declared in the manifest, so it's there before the view:

"sap.ui5": {
  "models": {
    "": { "type": "sap.ui.model.json.JSONModel", "uri": "model/data.json" }
  }
}

3. The view — the namespace peppie.control resolves to peppie/control/WebComponent.js via the resource root. Now binding is exactly what you'd hope for:

<mvc:View controllerName="peppie.controller.Main"
  xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc" xmlns="sap.m"
  xmlns:grid="sap.ui.layout.cssgrid" xmlns:wc="peppie.control">

  <VBox class="peppieWrap">

    <!-- a native UI5 control, bound to the same model -->
    <MessageStrip text="{/suggest}" type="{/suggestType}" showIcon="true"/>

    <!-- tiles: one control, four bindings, in a real CSS grid -->
    <grid:CSSGrid gridTemplateColumns="repeat(auto-fit, minmax(220px, 1fr))" gridGap="0.6rem">
      <wc:WebComponent tag="peppie-tile" data="{/tiles/0}"/>
      <wc:WebComponent tag="peppie-tile" data="{/tiles/1}"/>
      <wc:WebComponent tag="peppie-tile" data="{/tiles/2}"/>
      <wc:WebComponent tag="peppie-tile" data="{/tiles/3}"/>
    </grid:CSSGrid>

    <!-- cards: two columns that each stack tightly -->
    <HBox wrap="Wrap" class="peppieCols">
      <VBox class="peppieCol">
        <wc:WebComponent tag="peppie-flow"        data="{/flow}"/>
        <wc:WebComponent tag="peppie-metric-card" data="{/p1}"/>
        <!-- … taycan, ring -->
      </VBox>
      <VBox class="peppieCol">
        <wc:WebComponent tag="peppie-metric-card" data="{/solar}"/>
        <wc:WebComponent tag="peppie-metric-card" data="{/wp}"/>
        <!-- … tesla, hue -->
      </VBox>
    </HBox>

  </VBox>
</mvc:View>

One generic control wraps every element in the library. Ordinary UI5 layout does the arranging — a sap.ui.layout.cssgrid.CSSGrid for the tile row and two sap.m.VBox columns in a wrapping HBox for the cards (a float-based sap.ui.layout.Grid left ragged gaps between cards of different heights, so I switched). And a native sap.m.MessageStrip sits at the top, bound to the same model — proof that stock UI5 controls and Web Components mix freely in one view.

 

Running OpenUI5 app (sap_horizon_dark). The toolbar and its buttons are stock sap.m controls; everything below is Web Components bound to a JSONModel. Two worlds, one app.

The pitfalls (this is where the time goes)

1. Property, not attribute. The big one. UI5 binding, and most snippets you'll copy, write to attributes. Web Components take rich values — objects, arrays — as properties. setAttribute("data", {...}) stringifies to [object Object]. So in onAfterRendering you do element.data = this.getData(). That one line is why this works at all.

2. Bind objects, update them immutably. The wrapper binds a whole object (data="{/tiles/0}"), and JSONModel compares by reference. Mutate that object in place and the binding never fires. The tempting fix — model.refresh(true) — works, but it's a smell: it re-evaluates every binding in the app to paper over mutated state. The clean fix is to treat model data as immutable and replace the node with a new object, so the reference changes and exactly that binding updates:

onSimulate: function () {
  var m = this._oModel, oTile = m.getProperty("/tiles/0");
  // new object → new reference → the {/tiles/0} binding fires on its own
  m.setProperty("/tiles/0", Object.assign({}, oTile, { value: solar.toFixed(1) }));
}

No refresh(true), no shared state mutated in place — each changed node fires its own binding.

3. Re-render vs. live update. UI5 controls invalidate and re-render. You do not want to tear down and rebuild a custom element on every model tick. That's why setData uses suppressInvalidate = true and updates the live element directly. In the demo, "Simulate reading" changes the model and every tile, bar and card updates without a single re-render.

4. Load order and the upgrade trap. Define your custom elements before the control first renders. If UI5 renders <peppie-tile> before the browser knows the class, it's an inert unknown element; worse, setting .data on a not-yet-upgraded element creates an own-property that shadows the class setter after upgrade. Loading webcomponents.js in <head> before the bootstrap avoids the whole class of "it works on refresh but not first load" bugs.

5. Layout and height. Web Components are display: inline by default — render them display: block (the control does this). And don't drop them into a sap.m.Page that has no height: the Page positions its content absolutely, so a zero-height container renders a blank screen. I lost a minute to exactly that; a VBox/Grid that flows with content, or a container with a real height, fixes it.

6. Getting events back into UI5. Binding flows data in; for interaction out (a light toggled, a card clicked) let the element dispatch a CustomEvent and re-fire it as a UI5 event:

metadata: { events: { action: {} } },
onAfterRendering: function () {
  var oDom = this.getDomRef();
  oDom.data = this.getData();
  if (!this._wired) {                       // wire once
    oDom.addEventListener("peppie-action", (e) => this.fireAction({ detail: e.detail }));
    this._wired = true;
  }
}

Now <wc:WebComponent action=".onAction"/> works like any UI5 event handler.

The honest alternative

If you want to stay fully inside the SAP toolbox, you can: sap.f.Card for the layout, sap.suite.ui.microchart for the little charts. You get binding for free and a Fiori-native look. For this deliberately non-Fiori design, the Web Component route was less fighting and more reuse — the same components run outside UI5 too. Pick per project; just know both exist.

Try it

The full runnable demo is in ui5-demo/ — OpenUI5 from CDN, one wrapper control, a JSONModel, no build step. npx serve ui5-demo, open the URL, hit Simulate reading and Theme.

What this shows about building with AI

I described the goal — "wrap these custom elements as a data-bound UI5 control" — and the agent produced the control and got the app running fast, naming most pitfalls as we hit them: the attribute-vs-property gotcha, the sap.m.Page height trap, the upgrade-order bug. But not everything it produced was senior-grade. Its first fix for the object-binding problem was model.refresh(true) — and my own instinct said mutating model state and force-refreshing is not done. We replaced it with immutable setProperty updates. That's the real division of labour: the agent brings speed and breadth; you bring the judgement that turns working into clean. Treat its output as a strong first draft, not the final commit.

Your turn — a give-away

This demo is JavaScript. It should be TypeScript: the model typed, the control's managed properties typed, the Web Components' data typed end to end. That's a real, multi-file refactor across the UI5 Tooling — exactly the kind of well-scoped job an agent is good at and you review.

I ran this prompt myself before publishing it, and it turned out to be the whole series' thesis in one artifact. Left vague, the agent produces something that looks converted but doesn't build: a broken generator dependency (@ui5/ts-interface-generator pulls in an ESM-only yargs and won't run), the OpenUI5 framework config the CDN app never had, a setData signature that clashes with the generated interface, a type-only module that gets stripped to a non-module. So the prompt below is deliberately specific — exact versions, and the three real blockers baked in. That specificity is the difference between "the agent tried" and the build is green.

Run it in the ui5-demo/ folder and hold it to the acceptance criteria at the end.

You are a senior SAPUI5 + TypeScript engineer. Convert the JavaScript UI5 app in this folder to
TypeScript — fully typed, strict:true — and make it BUILD and SERVE with the UI5 Tooling. Do NOT
change runtime behaviour; the app must render and behave exactly as it does now. Work in small,
reviewable steps: layout & framework first, then toolchain, then types, then module conversion.

0. Environment. @ui5/cli 4 wants Node >= 22.12; older Node only prints an EBADENGINE warning and
   still works.

1. Layout & framework — the app currently loads OpenUI5 from a CDN; that must change, because the
   UI5 Tooling serves the framework itself.
   - Move all app sources under webapp/: index.html, Component.ts, manifest.json, controller/,
     view/, control/, model/, and the web-components module.
   - Add ui5.yaml that pins OpenUI5 locally (no CDN):
       specVersion: "3.0"
       metadata: { name: peppie }
       type: application
       framework:
         name: OpenUI5
         version: "1.120.46"
         libraries:
           - name: sap.ui.core
           - name: sap.m
           - name: sap.ui.layout
           - name: themelib_sap_horizon      # required for sap_horizon_dark
       builder:
         customTasks:
           - name: ui5-tooling-transpile-task
             afterTask: replaceVersion
       server:
         customMiddleware:
           - name: ui5-tooling-transpile-middleware
             afterMiddleware: compression
   - In webapp/index.html change the bootstrap src from the CDN URL to "resources/sap-ui-core.js"
     (served by the Tooling). Keep data-sap-ui-theme="sap_horizon_dark", the ComponentSupport
     oninit, and data-sap-ui-resourceroots='{"peppie": "./"}'. Ensure manifest.json sap.app.id =
     "peppie" so the resourceroots, the /** @namespace peppie.* */ values, and the manifest id all
     agree.

2. Toolchain & exact, known-good versions.
   devDependencies:
     "@openui5/types": "1.120.46"
     "@ui5/cli": "^4.0.57"
     "@ui5/ts-interface-generator": "^0.11.1"
     "typescript": "^5.9.3"
     "ui5-tooling-transpile": "^3.11.3"
   overrides (MANDATORY — see step 4):
     "@ui5/ts-interface-generator": { "yargs": "^17.7.2" }
   scripts:
     "ts:check": "tsc --noEmit"
     "build": "ui5 build --clean-dest"
     "start": "ui5 serve --open index.html"
   tsconfig.json: strict:true, target/module "ES2022", moduleResolution "node",
     lib ["ES2022","DOM","DOM.Iterable"], skipLibCheck:true, rootDir "webapp",
     paths { "peppie/*": ["webapp/*"] }, types ["@openui5/types"],
     include ["webapp/**/*.ts","webapp/**/*.d.ts"].

3. Convert each sap.ui.define([...], function(){}) into an ES-module TypeScript class with
   import / export default, UI5 TS style, keeping the namespace via /** @namespace peppie.control */.

4. The custom control — mind the interface generator.
   - Keep the metadata block; type the renderer (RenderManager), onAfterRendering, and the setData
     override.
   - Run @ui5/ts-interface-generator to emit WebComponent.gen.d.ts (typed getTag/setTag/
     getData/setData plus $WebComponentSettings), and make sure tsconfig includes it.
   - BLOCKER: @ui5/ts-interface-generator@0.11.1 require()s yargs but resolves the ESM-only
     yargs@18 -> ERR_REQUIRE_ESM, so the generator won't run at all. The overrides pin in step 2
     (yargs ^17.7.2) fixes it. Install that BEFORE running the generator.
   - Two things the generator will NOT do for you:
       a) It prints three constructor(...) overload lines and says "copy & paste manually" — paste
          them into the class (referencing $WebComponentSettings). It never edits your source.
       b) It also generates setData(data: object | null): this. Your hand-written setData MUST use
          the exact same `object | null` parameter type, or declaration-merging fails with an
          overload-compatibility error.

5. Types. Add webapp/model/types.ts with interfaces TileData, FlowRow, FlowData, MetricItem,
   MetricCardData, EvCardData, RingCam, RingCardData, LightGroup, LightsCardData, and a root
   DashboardModel including suggest:string and
   suggestType:"Success"|"Warning"|"Error"|"Information"|"None". Type the JSONModel and the
   controller's onSimulate against DashboardModel.

6. The web components — make it a real UI5 module.
   - Write webcomponents.ts with a typed `set data(d: …)` per element and augment
     HTMLElementTagNameMap so createElement("peppie-tile") and the control's getDomRef() are typed.
   - IMPORTANT: add at least one runtime `export` (e.g. export { PeppieTile, PeppieFlow, … }). A
     module with only TYPE exports gets type-stripped to a plain script and is NOT wrapped as a
     sap.ui.define AMD module — the build then warns it "requires eval". A real runtime export
     makes it a proper module.
   - Have Component.ts do a side-effect import "./webcomponents"; so the custom elements are defined
     before the controls render.
   - In the wrapper, type the element so assigning .data is checked. A single, commented
     `getDomRef() as HTMLElement & { data: unknown }` cast is fine — do not use `any`.

Acceptance criteria (all must pass):
- `npm run ts:check` -> zero errors with strict:true.
- `npm run build` (ui5 build) -> green, no warnings, produces a runnable build.
- `npm start` (ui5 serve) -> renders identically: four tiles, two-column cards, the MessageStrip,
  and "Simulate reading" flipping the strip between Warning and Success.
- The generic WebComponent wrapper still wraps any element through one `data` property; no `any`
  anywhere (the one commented getDomRef() cast excepted).

When done, show me the final tsconfig.json, ui5.yaml, package.json, the typed WebComponent, and
model/types.ts.

Validated: this produced tsc --noEmit with zero strict errors, a green ui5 build, and a ui5 serve render pixel-identical to the JavaScript original. First draft from the agent; the specificity — and the final commit — from you.

Next

The house is connected (Part 1) and it has a real UI5 frontend (Part 2). In Part 3 I pull back the curtain on how most of this actually got built: an AI agent that read a login captcha, hacked a solar inverter, and then deleted its own hack when the clean route opened up.

Part 3 — I let an AI agent connect the whole house — is next.

entry.title: Code Connect 2026: Final Info Before We Kick Off!
entry.content:
Code Connect 2026 is just around the corner! From July 13–16, 2026, the SAP developer community will gather in St. Leon-Rot, Germany – onsite and online.

Here’s everything you need to know before we get started.

 

 

We Are Fully Booked!

All onsite tickets are fully booked - thank you for the amazing interest!

If you couldn’t secure a ticket:

  • Join the waiting lists on Code Connect 2026 in case seats become available.
  • Follow selected sessions via live stream or Microsoft Teams link.

Even if you're not onsite, you can still be part of Code Connect. Selected sessions will be streamed live, and recordings of some sessions will be available afterwards. Check back soon for details.

 

 

 

UI5con (July 14)

  • Main stage sessions will be live streamed on YouTube.
  • Check the agenda to see which sessions are streamed.

 

 

re>≡CAP (July 15)

  • Main stage sessions will be broadcasted.
  • Some sidetracks (W1/W2) can be attended via Microsoft Teams link.
  • Check the agenda to see which sessions are broadcasted or available via Microsoft Teams link.

 

 

HANA Tech Con (July 16)

  • The links will be announced on the event day.

 

The Agendas Are Live

The full program is already available - time to plan your schedule!

Check-In opens at 8.00 AM every day. Please bring the QR code with you that you can find in your ticket.

July 13 – Code Jams & Community Meetup

  • Hands-on Code Jams:
    • OpenUI5
    • CAP
    • AI Agents
  • Informal pre-event meetup (no registration needed):
    Ihle Besen, Höfe am Sträßel 3, 69231 Rauenberg
    5:00 PM CEST

July 14–16 – Main Conference Days

Explore all agendas:

  • July 14: UI5con
  • July 15: re>≡CAP
  • July 16: HANA Tech Con

On July 14 also the HANA AI CodeJam takes place.

 

Location

Code Connect takes place at:

SAP-Allee 27
68789 St. Leon-Rot, Germany

Find detailed information on directions, parking, and public transport here.

 

Final Tips

  • Bookmark your favorite sessions in advance
  • Arrive early for popular sessions (rooms fill quickly!)
  • Bring your laptop for Code Jams and hands-on sessions/ workshops
  • Don’t miss the networking moments - some of the best conversations happen in between sessions
  • Follow the agendas closely for last-minute updates

 

See You Soon!

We’re looking forward to an inspiring week of learning, sharing, and connecting.

See you at Code Connect 2026!

entry.title: SAP Fiori Development newsletter issue #41 (July 2026)
entry.content:
Summer is here and it’s getting hot. I’m not just talking about the weather. AI is heating up both for the companies that create the large language models and everyone trying to keep up with the latest innovations. All while managing their token costs. SAP is embracing AI for our internal application development and the tools we provide customers and partners for building their own applications.

Sapphire produced several major announcements around our development tools, the biggest being Joule Studio 2.0. Joule Studio 2.0 is SAP’s AI-first development environment for building custom AI agents, apps, and workflows. Powered by the SAP Business AI Platform.

As the development landscape shifts, we want to know what’s important to you. We are interested in how well SAPUI5 meets your needs today, which UI experiences you expect to dominate enterprise apps in the coming years, and which capabilities matter most for your future AI-powered development work. Please take this 5-minute survey.

If you want to join other developers to discuss various traditional and AI-powered Fiori development-related topics from the comfort of your office or home, I encourage you to join our monthly SAP Fiori development roundtable. Register online or email me for an invitation.

Development News

Optimizing SAP Fiori Elements for Mobile Phone Users

While SAP Fiori elements applications are responsive, they adjust controls based on screen size and device type, it does not provide an optimized experience for users. We recently reimagined the SAP Fiori elements mobile experience to include native mobile feel, intuitive navigation, and smarter mobile-optimized layouts. Read more.

Develop RAP-Based SAP Fiori Applications in Minutes with SAP Fiori Tools AI Project Accelerator

Developing enterprise SAP Fiori applications using the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) provides a standardized way to build modern, cloud-ready applications. These applications are typically developed in environments such as SAP BTP ABAP Environment and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (version 2602 or higher). The Project Accelerator now supports RAP-based projects. It is available in SAP Business Application Studio under an SAP Build Code subscription. Learn more.

TypeScript 6 and 7 – What UI5 TypeScript Developers Need to Know in 2026

TypeScript is going through its biggest transition in years — and if you build UI5 applications with TypeScript, here we have all the details you need to know. Read about it in this blog post.

UI5 Plugins for Coding Agents: Now Supporting GitHub Copilot

The UI5 Plugins for Coding Agents — previously known as UI5 Plugins for Claude — are now also available for GitHub Copilot. The plugins bring up-to-date SAPUI5 and OpenUI5 knowledge directly into your AI-assisted development workflow, whether you work with Claude Code or GitHub Copilot. Version 0.1.3 also introduces three new skills covering UI Integration Cards, OPA5 integration tests, and OpenUI5 tables. Read more.

Introducing the UI5 Modernization Plugin

Keeping your SAPUI5 or OpenUI5 app up to date just got easier. The new UI5 Modernization Plugin automates the most time-consuming parts of app modernization: replacing deprecated APIs, eliminating global namespaces, updating manifest.json and Component.js, and getting your app ready for Content Security Policy compliance. The plugin is available as a Claude Code plugin and in the GitHub Copilot marketplace. Read the full walkthrough.

 What's next for UI technology in the age of AI?

We're running a short survey to collect input from developers, architects, and UI technology enthusiasts in the SAP ecosystem — and we want to hear from you. Topics include how well SAPUI5 meets your needs today, how AI tools work with your current UI stack, and which capabilities matter most for your future development work. Shape the SAPUI5 road map by answering few questions in just 5 minutes, open July 1–19. Take the survey.

Joule Studio 2.0 announced at Sapphire

SAP announced its latest development environment for building apps, agents, and workflows. Read how it changes how you build apps.

SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities including agentic capabilities is now available!

ABAP AI in SAP Joule for Developers include agentic capabilities and is now offered as a service across SAP BTP ABAP Environment, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition for releases 2021 and higher. Joule speaks ABAP, and now it speaks more than ever before. See the details.

Upgrading your SAPUI5 Rich Text Editor: Beyond TinyMCE version 4

The Rich Text Editor is an SAPUI5 control for data input and editing. It is used to enter rich text with different styles, colors, and formatting. The Rich Text Editor allows users to format text and add different types of elements, such as images and hyperlinks. he SAPUI5 team further plans to permanently remove TinyMCE version 4 from the SAPUI5 code later in 2026. Learn how to adjust your code to avoid any problems.

Events

The future of UX is here. Are you ready for an AI -powered world?

At Sapphire 2026 I had the opportunity to speak with many customers and partners about their reaction to the way we are moving with our UX strategy. Generally, people are excited about the direction we are going and having more automation to free up time for deeper thought and analysis, but they are concerned about trusting AI to do things that might have significant business impact. Read my full analysis.

SAP Connect Day for UX in Silicon Valley focused on AI strategy and tactics for implementation

SAP Connect Day for UX in Silicon Valley connected customers with SAP experts and selected partners to discuss how AI will improve the lives of SAP Cloud ERP users. Read about highlights and where you can learn more about the topics we discussed.

Back issues from the past year

SAP Fiori Development Newsletter issue #40 (May 2026)

SAP Fiori Development newsletter March 2026 (issue #39)

SAP Fiori development newsletter January 2026 (issue #38)

SAP Fiori development newsletter November 2025 (issue #37)

SAP Fiori development newsletter September 2025 (issue #36)

SAP Fiori development newsletter July 2025 (issue #35)

entry.title: Adding a Custom Column in Fiori Elements (List Report + Object Page)
entry.content:
Introduction 

The purpose of this blog post is to specify the elements to add in order to set up this custom column, and more specifically to understand the path to configure in the manifest when you want to modify a table, because using this extension in an association facet of an Object Page can be more complicated to understand.
To do so, two elements must be implemented: first, the column definition through an XML fragment, and second, its registration in the manifest file.

I had to use this extension because I was asked to customize the data display by applying CSS styles, such as changing the color depending on the displayed value.
This blog post also aims to specify when to use this extension and why it is preferable not to use it, along with an example of a regression I experienced during its addition.

This column can be added either to a List Report page or to an Object Page. Each table type has its own extension name, and the implementation differs slightly depending on the table type.

       Why use this extension as a last resort?

In this blog post, we will look at how to add a custom column. However, other approaches can often meet the requirement. This extension should be considered only when no standard alternative is available, for the following reasons:

  • Fiori Elements Flexibility: Some requirements can be fulfilled through manifest customization or OData annotations without writing any JavaScript or XML code.
  • Maintainability: View extensions are custom code that must be maintained whenever the Fiori Elements framework is updated, whereas standard annotations are automatically handled by SAP.
  • Regression Risk: A poorly implemented extension can interfere with native table features such as sorting, filtering, Excel export, and P13n personalization.

Example of a regression I encountered with this extension:

While developing my XML, I needed to add a specific width to my column. However, by defining this width in pixels (px), I caused a regression in the native behavior, which uses rem units. For example, the display variants were altered depending on the screen size, because rem units rely on a relative value, unlike pixels which impose a fixed size.

      Table Types and Associated Extensions

Fiori Elements supports several table types. Each has its own extension name that must be declared in the manifest.

Table Type 

Extension Name 

Description

ResponsiveTable

ResponsiveTableColumnsExtension

ResponsiveTableCellsExtension

Responsive table adapted for mobile and desktop (default)

GridTable

GridTableColumnsExtension

Table with fixed columns, suitable for large datasets

TreeTable

TreeTableColumnsExtension

Hierarchical (tree-like) table

Important: For a ResponsiveTable, two extensions are required: one for the column header and one for the cell content.

How to Implement the Column?

     XML Extension

To add this column, you need to define a fragment for the view extension. Create a file in the webapp/ext/fragments/ folder to add any desired column.

In this fragment, you define the desired properties of the column (position, displayed element, etc.). You can also add P13N parameters, such as sorting, to enable users to sort the column.

Example XML extension:

 

      Manifest Extension

In the manifest.json file, declare the view extension and the application parameters. Create an extension corresponding to the table type on the desired entity, then specify the fragment name and type.

Example Manifest:

Result :

After adding the XML extension and declaring it in the manifest.json, a new column appears in the table. In the example, the column customizes data display by applying CSS styles, such as changing colors based on displayed values.

The document also highlights a date-coloring scenario where different colors indicate whether a deadline is distant, approaching, or overdue. In such cases, a view extension is generally unnecessary because OData Criticality annotations can provide the same functionality in a standard, Fiori Elements–compliant way. 

Therefore, this standard approach should be preferred whenever they satisfy the requirement.

Adding a Column to an Object Page

      Differences Compared to a List Report

An Object Page can contain multiple sections, each with its own table. The main differences are:

Aspect

List Report

Object Page

Target View 

ListReport.view.ListReport

ObjectPage.view.Details

Extension key

TypeTable + '|' + EntitySet

TypeTable + '|' + EntitySet + FacetID

Number of Tables 

One main table

Multiple tables (one per section)

Configuration Scope 

Entity-wide

Specific to each section/facet

      Example in an Object Page

In this example, a custom column is added to a Responsive Table within the Object Page of the Category entity. 

The custom column contains an Order button that is displayed only when the product stock is below 30 units. This behavior relies on business logic that cannot be implemented using standard Fiori Elements annotations. 

As with the Grid Table example, an extension fragment is created for the column definition:

However, because this is a Responsive Table, a second extension is required to define the column header:

Since the extension is used within an Object Page, the manifest.json configuration differs slightly from a List Report. You must target the ObjectPage.view.Details view and specify the corresponding facet in the extension key to indicate which table should receive the custom column.

The point I spent time on due to a lack of understanding: 'TypeTable' corresponds to the CDS of our table and not the CDS of the Object Page. In our example, 'Products' is a CDS association (added in a 'ProductsFacet' facet) of our application, which is based on the 'Category' CDS.


Result: 

 

Purpose of the Extension

     When Should You Use This Extension?

This extension is relevant in the following scenarios:

  • Complex conditional display: color, icon, or text based on multi-field business logic that cannot be expressed through annotations.
  • Non-standard UI5 component: action button, link, image, slider, or any component not natively supported by UI.DataField.
  • External data integration: displaying data coming from another JSON model or a separate REST API call. 
  • Frontend-side calculation: combining or calculating values using a JavaScript formatter. 

     Best Practices 

  • Always prefix IDs: avoid conflicts with IDs generated by Fiori Elements. 
  • Use i18n: never hardcode header texts; always use entries from the i18n.properties file.
  • Keep JavaScript out of fragments: complex logic should be implemented in a dedicated formatter.js file, not inline in the XML.
  • Test P13n (personalization): ensure that the custom column behaves correctly with sorting, filtering, and show/hide functionalities.

entry.title: Getting Started with GitHub in SAP Business Application Studio for SAP UI5 Development
entry.content:
Overview:

SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) trial accounts expire every 90 days, making it essential to safeguard SAP UI5 projects using a reliable version control system. GitHub not only helps prevent project loss but also introduces the collaboration practices widely used in professional software development.

This blog covers:

  • Connecting SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) with GitHub
  • Step-by-step process to push and manage SAP UI5 projects using GitHub.

The intent is to provide a simple and practical guide for SAP UI5 learners and developers to protect their projects, understand Git and GitHub fundamentals, and adopt industry-standard version control practices.

Introduction:

When I started learning SAP UI5, I was using a trial account on SAP Business Application Studio (BAS). 

Everything was going great — until I hit my first big obstacle. 

  • Every 90 days, the trial account expires. And when it does, everything is gone. All my projects. All my code. Vanished. 

My only option back then was to manually download each project as a zip file to my desktop before the account reset. Then when the new trial started, I had to upload everything back again. 

It was painful. It was repetitive. And one time, I forgot — and lost a project I had worked on for weeks. 

That's when I discovered GitHub — and it completely changed how I work.

I started learning how to:

  • Upload my SAP UI5 projects from SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) to GitHub.
  • Access them anytime, from anywhere, even after my BAS trial account resets.
  • Share my projects easily with teammates and collaborate more effectively.
  • No more downloading ZIP files. No more worrying about the 90-day deadline. My code was safely stored in the cloud, always accessible, and backed by version control.

What Is GitHub and Why Do We Need It?

The easiest way to think about GitHub is this: it's like Google Docs for source code. Instead of collaborating on documents, developers collaborate on code while Git keeps track of every change.

GitHub is a cloud-based platform built on top of Git, a distributed version control system. It enables developers to store their source code in the cloud, track changes over time, and collaborate with others while maintaining a complete history of every modification.

In addition to version control, GitHub provides features such as repositories, branches, pull requests, and code reviews, making it easier for individuals and teams to manage software projects efficiently.

Although GitHub is widely used for open-source development, it is equally important in enterprise environments. Development teams use it to collaborate on projects, review code, manage changes, and maintain high-quality software throughout the development lifecycle.

GitHub is not just a tool for beginners. It is an industry-standard platform used by organizations of all sizes to support real-world software development and team collaboration.

In real-world software projects, developers rarely work alone. Front-end developers, backend developers, testers, and other team members often work on the same application simultaneously. Without a version control platform like GitHub, collaboration can quickly become difficult, with developers accidentally overwriting each other's changes and having no clear way to track who changed what or revert to previous versions.

GitHub solves this challenge through features such as branching. Think of the main project as a tree trunk, where each developer creates their own branch to work independently on a feature, enhancement, or bug fix. Once the work is complete, the changes are reviewed through a Pull Request (PR) and then merged back into the main branch.

This approach enables multiple developers to work in parallel without interfering with one another, maintains a complete history of code changes, and ensures that every update is reviewed before becoming part of the main project. As a result, GitHub has become an industry-standard platform for version control and team collaboration in professional software development.

A Typical Team Workflow
A typical GitHub workflow in a development team looks like this:

 
main branch          → Stable, production-ready code

├── developer-1      → Working on the Login feature
├── developer-2      → Working on the Dashboard feature
└── developer-3      → Fixing a bug in the Search feature

Each developer works on their own branch without affecting the work of others. Once a feature or bug fix is complete, the developer creates a Pull Request (PR) to request that their changes be reviewed before they are merged into the main branch.

A senior developer or technical lead reviews the code, provides feedback if any changes are required, and approves the Pull Request once the code meets the project's quality standards. After approval, the changes are merged into the main branch.

This workflow helps ensure:

  • Developers can work independently without interfering with one another.
  • Every code change is reviewed before being merged.

A complete history of changes is maintained, making it easy to track who changed what and when.

The main branch remains stable and ready for deployment.

 Now, let's walk through the step-by-step process of connecting SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) to GitHub and uploading your SAP UI5 project.

Step- 1: Create a GitHub Account

  • Sign up for a GitHub account if you don't already have one.

Step 2: Initialize the Git Repository

  • Open your SAP UI5 project in SAP Business Application Studio (BAS).
  • Open the Terminal by navigating to Terminal → New Terminal.

    Run the following command to initialize a Git repository with main as the default branch:
git init -b main​

This command initializes a new Git repository in your project directory and creates the default branch named main. Git will now start tracking changes made to your project.

Step 3: Stage All Project Files

  • After initializing the Git repository, stage all the project files by running the following command in the terminal:
git add .​
  • The git add . command stages all the files and folders in the current project directory. Staging tells Git which changes should be included in the next commit.
  • Once the files are staged, Git is ready to create a snapshot of your project using a commit.
  • Note: The . (dot) represents the current directory, so git add . stages all files within your project.

Step 4: Commit the Changes

  • Once all the project files have been staged, create your first commit by running the following command:
git commit -m "Vendor Details"​
  • Creates a commit with the message "Vendor Details", which serves as a snapshot of your project at its current state.
  • Verify the Commit
  • To confirm that the commit was created successfully, run the following command:
  • git log
  • Displays the commit history of the repository, including the commit ID, author, date, and commit message.
  • If the commit was successful, you should see an output similar to:
commit 3f2c9b8e6d4f1a2b...
Author: <Your Name> <your-email@example.com>
Date:   Sat Jun 6 10:30:15 2026

    Vendor Details​
  • This confirms that your project has been committed successfully and Git is now tracking this version of your code.

Step 5: Configure Git (First-Time Setup)

  • If you're using Git for the first time, configure your username and email address using the following commands:
git config --global user.name "<github_username>"
git config --global user.email "<github_email>"
  • Configures your Git username and email address. These details are associated with every commit you create.

Step 6: Create a GitHub Repository

  • Sign in to your GitHub account.
  • Click New Repository.
  • Enter a repository name (for example, Vendor_Details_App).
  • Click Create repository.

Step 7: Connect the Local Repository to GitHub

  • After creating the repository, copy its HTTPS URL and run the following command in the BAS terminal:
git remote add origin https://github.com/sap-honey/Vendor_Details_App.git
  • Connects your local Git repository to the remote GitHub repository.

Step 8: Push the Project to GitHub

  • Push your local commits to the remote GitHub repository using the following command:
git push -u origin main​
  • Uploads your local main branch to the remote GitHub repository. The -u option sets the upstream branch, so future pushes can be performed simply by running:
git push​

  • When you run the git push command for the first time, GitHub prompts you to authenticate your account.

  • Click Copy and Open GitHub.

  • This copies the verification code and opens the GitHub device activation page in your browser.
  • GitHub automatically opens the device activation page. If prompted, sign in to your GitHub account.
  • Paste the verification code and click Continue, then click Authorize to grant SAP Business Application Studio permission to access your GitHub repository.
  • After the authorization is complete, return to SAP Business Application Studio. The git push command will finish automatically.
  • Open your GitHub account and navigate to the repository you created.
  • Refresh the repository page. You should now see all your SAP UI5 project files successfully uploaded.

    This confirms that your local SAP UI5 project has been successfully pushed to GitHub. From now on, you only need to commit your changes and run:

git push​

GitHub is an essential tool for every SAP UI5 developer. By connecting SAP Business Application Studio with GitHub, you can securely store your projects, track changes, and continue your work even after a BAS trial reset.

I hope this blog helps you get started with GitHub and makes managing your SAP UI5 projects easier and more efficient. Happy coding!

entry.title: How to Add a Custom Formatter in SAPUI5: View vs. Controller Approach
entry.content:
Hi Everyone,

Introduction

When building SAPUI5 applications, we often bind data directly from a model to the UI. While simple property binding works well for displaying raw values, there are many scenarios where the displayed value needs additional formatting before it reaches the user. For example, you might want to combine multiple fields into a single sentence, display semantic colors based on a value, or convert technical data into a more user-friendly format.

This is where custom formatters become useful. They allow you to keep formatting logic separate from your XML views, making your application easier to read, maintain, and reuse.

In this blog, we'll explore:

  • What a custom formatter is and why you should use one.
  • Two different ways to integrate an external formatter into your SAPUI5 application:
    • Using core:require directly in the XML View.
    • Importing the formatter through the Controller.
  • A practical example that demonstrates both approaches side by side using real code and application output.
  • When to use each approach, so you can choose the one that best fits your application's structure and development style.

Project Context

While practicing SAPUI5, I wanted to organize my formatting logic outside the XML View instead of writing formatting code directly in bindings. During this exercise, I discovered that SAPUI5 provides more than one way to use external formatter files.

I experimented with both approaches, loading the formatter directly in the XML View using core:require and importing it through the controller. Since both approaches produce the same result but differ in how the formatter is referenced, I thought it would be useful to compare them side by side with a simple practical example.

What is a Custom Formatter, and Why Use One

A custom formatter is a JavaScript function that transforms model data before it is displayed in the UI. It allows you to present data in a more meaningful or user-friendly format without modifying the original data in the model. Since a formatter only affects the displayed value, it supports one-way binding and cannot be used for two-way (editable) bindings.

Some common use cases include:

  • Converting technical values into user-friendly text.
  • Combining multiple model properties into a single display value.
  • Applying semantic states such as Success, Warning, or Error based on a value.
  • Formatting dates, currency, or performing simple calculations before displaying data.

In the following sections, we'll implement these concepts with a practical SAPUI5 example using reusable external formatter files.

Project Setup and Folder Structure

Here's the folder structure used for this example, a simple table of users with their age and salary, where we'll apply one formatter for each column:

project1/                        ← SAPUI5 application
└── webapp/
    ├── controller/               ← Controller logic
    │   ├── App.controller.js
    │   └── View1.controller.js   ← attaches AgeFormatter (controller approach)
    ├── formatter/                ← Reusable external formatter files
    │   ├── AgeFormatter.js       ← used via controller
    │   └── SalaryFormatter.js    ← used via core:require in the view
    ├── model/
    │   ├── models.js
    │   └── users.json            ← sample data
    ├── view/
    │   ├── App.view.xml
    │   └── View1.view.xml        ← table using both formatter approaches
    ├── Component.js
    ├── index.html
    └── manifest.json             ← model & routing configuration

For this example, I created two separate formatter files to demonstrate both approaches independently. One formatter is loaded directly in the XML View using core:require, while the other is imported through the controller. This makes it easier to understand how each approach works without mixing the implementations.

  • AgeFormatter.js – Combines a user's name and age into a readable sentence.
  • SalaryFormatter.js – Maps a salary value to an appropriate semantic state.

Sample Data (users.json)

The application uses a simple JSON model containing user information. The age and salary properties are used by the formatter functions in the following examples.

{
  "users": [
    { "id": 1, "name": "Monkey D. Luffy", "age": 16, "salary": 22000 },
    { "id": 2, "name": "Roronoa Zoro", "age": 21, "salary": 64000 },
    { "id": 3, "name": "Nami", "age": 20, "salary": 2400 },
    { "id": 4, "name": "Usopp", "age": 19, "salary": 2500 },
    { "id": 5, "name": "Sanji", "age": 21, "salary": 7000 },
    { "id": 6, "name": "Tony Tony Chopper", "age": 17, "salary": 6800 },
    { "id": 7, "name": "Nico Robin", "age": 30, "salary": 9200 },
    { "id": 8, "name": "Franky", "age": 36, "salary": 88000 },
    { "id": 9, "name": "Brook", "age": 90, "salary": 33000 },
    { "id": 10, "name": "Jinbe", "age": 46, "salary": 440000 }
  ]
}

Registering the Model (manifest.json)

"oModel": {
    "type": "sap.ui.model.json.JSONModel",
    "uri": "model/users.json"
}

The JSON model is registered in manifest.json, making it available throughout the application using the model name oModel.

Approach 1: Using core:require in the View

This approach loads the formatter module directly in the XML view using the core:require attribute, and gives it an alias you can reference in any binding inside that view. No changes are needed in the controller.

SalaryFormatter.js

sap.ui.define([], function(){
    "use strict";
    return {
         /*
         * This formatter is required directly in the VIEW via core:require
         * (see View1.view.xml) and referenced as 'Salary.salaryState'.
         */
        salaryState: function(salary){
           if (salary >= 10000) {
                return "Success"; // green color
            } else if (salary >= 5000) {
                return "Warning"; // orange color
            } else {
                return "Error";   // red color
            }
        }
    }
})

While implementing this example, I kept the salary-related formatting in a dedicated formatter file instead of writing conditional logic directly in the XML View. This keeps the binding expression clean and makes the formatting logic reusable.

View1.view.xml

<mvc:View controllerName="project1.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m"
    xmlns:core="sap.ui.core"
    core:require="{ Salary: 'project1/formatter/SalaryFormatter' }">
    <!-- Approach 1 (view-based):
         core:require loads the SalaryFormatter module and makes it available
         under the alias "Salary" for use anywhere inside this view. -->
    <Page id="page" title="{i18n>title}">
        <Table items="{oModel>/users}">
            <columns>
                <Column><Text text="ID" /></Column>
                <Column><Text text="Name" /></Column>
                <Column><Text text="Age" /></Column>
                <Column><Text text="Salary" /></Column>
                <Column><Text text="Age Classification" /></Column>
            </columns>
            <items>
                <ColumnListItem>
                    <Text text="{oModel>id}" />
                    <Text text="{oModel>name}" />
                    <Text text="{oModel>age}" />

                    <!-- "Salary" here refers to the alias declared in core:require above -->
                    <ObjectStatus text="{oModel>salary}" state="{path:'oModel>salary', formatter:'Salary.salaryState'}" />
                </ColumnListItem>
            </items>
        </Table>
    </Page>
</mvc:View>

One thing I found convenient about this approach is that everything required by the formatter stays inside the XML View. Since the formatter is only used in this view, there was no need to modify the controller. This also keeps the formatter dependency close to where it is actually used.

Approach 2: Attaching the Formatter in the Controller

While exploring formatter implementations, I also tried importing the formatter through the controller. In this approach, the formatter is loaded as a controller dependency and then exposed to the XML View through the formatter property.

AgeFormatter.js

sap.ui.define([], function () {
    "use strict";

    return {
        // Classifies a person's age into a category and returns a readable sentence.
        ageClassification: function (sName, sAge) {
            var nAge = Number(sAge);
            var sCategory;

            if (nAge < 13) {
                sCategory = "Child";
            } else if (nAge < 18) {
                sCategory = "Youth";
            } else if (nAge < 60) {
                sCategory = "Adult";
            } else {
                sCategory = "Senior Citizen";
            }

            // Pick "a" or "an" based on whether the category starts with a vowel sound
            var sArticle = /^[aeiou]/i.test(sCategory) ? "an" : "a";

            return `${sName} is ${sArticle} ${sCategory}`;
        }
    };
});

View1.controller.js

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller",
    // Import the formatter module here and attach it to the controller
    "project1/formatter/AgeFormatter"
], (Controller, AgeFormatter) => {
    "use strict";
    return Controller.extend("project1.controller.View1", {

        // Attaching the module as "formatter" is what makes
        // ".formatter.ageClassification" resolvable from the view
        formatter: AgeFormatter,

        onInit() {
        }
    });
});
View1.view.xml (relevant part)
<!-- The leading dot means "look this up on the controller instance",
     i.e. this.formatter.ageClassification -->
<Text text="{parts:[{path:'oModel>name'}, {path:'oModel>age'}], formatter:'.formatter.ageClassification'}" />

Only the binding expression changes in the XML View. The remaining XML structure stays exactly the same as in the previous approach, so only the relevant binding is shown below.

Figure 1: XML View showing where each formatter approach is used

After implementing both approaches, I noticed that the application behavior and output remain exactly the same. The only difference is how the formatter module is made available to the XML View. This observation helped me understand that the choice is primarily about code organization rather than functionality.

Putting Both Together

Here's the same app with both formatters wired up, salary status via the view-based approach, age classification via the controller-based approach, running against a simple JSON model:

Figure 2: Output showing salary status and age classification for all users

Both approaches produce correct, working output. The difference is purely in where the dependency is declared and how it's referenced.

View vs. Controller Approach

Both approaches ultimately achieve the same goal. They allow you to keep formatting logic outside the XML View and produce the same output. After implementing both in the same application, I noticed that the primary difference isn't what they do, but where the formatter dependency is managed.

With the View approach (core:require), the formatter is imported directly into the XML View.
