This makes the view self-contained because anyone reading the XML can immediately see which formatter modules it depends on. I found this approach straightforward when the formatter is only used within a single view.

With the Controller approach, the formatter is imported as a controller dependency and then exposed to the XML View through the formatter property. This keeps all module imports together inside the controller, which can make the XML slightly cleaner. I found this approach useful because the controller becomes the single place responsible for managing its dependencies.

Another thing I observed is that the formatter implementation itself doesn't change. The same formatter function can be used with either approach. Only the way SAPUI5 resolves and accesses that function is different.

From a functionality perspective, both approaches produce identical results. The choice mainly comes down to how you prefer to organize your application and how your project structures reusable modules.

Conclusion

While building this example, I realized that both approaches ultimately achieve the same result. The difference lies in how the formatter dependency is organized rather than how the formatter behaves. Implementing and comparing both approaches side by side gave me a better understanding of how SAPUI5 resolves formatter functions, and I hope this practical comparison helps you make the right choice for your own applications.

Thank you for reading! If you've used a different approach or have additional insights, I'd love to hear about them in the comments.

entry.title: How To Create Value Help In CAPM.
entry.content:
Hi Everyone,

In this blog, I'll walk you through one of the easiest ways to implement Value Help (F4 Help) in a SAP CAPM application.

While developing SAP Fiori applications, we often have fields such as Category, Department, Country, Employee, Supplier, and many others where users should select values instead of typing them manually. If users enter values manually, there is always a chance of entering incorrect or inconsistent data.

To solve this problem, SAP provides Value Help (F4 Help), which allows users to choose values from a predefined list. This not only improves the user experience but also ensures that only valid data is saved.

In this blog, I'll demonstrate how to create a simple Value Help for the Category field by using a lookup entity, exposing it through a CAP service, and configuring the required CDS annotations.

To Demonstrate this I have Created a simple CAPM application, folder structure of this Looks like this below image



Step 1 – Create Database Entities

First, open the db folder and create a schema.cds file.

In this example, I created two entities:

  • Products
  • Categories

The Products entity stores the product details, while the Categories entity acts as the lookup table that provides the values for the Value Help.

Below is the content of my schema.cds file.

namespace demo.valuehelp;

entity Products {
  key ID       : UUID;
  name         : String(100);
  category     : String(10);
  price        : Decimal(10,2);
}

entity Categories {
  key code     : String(10);
  description  : String(50);
}

Step 2 – Create Sample Data

Next, inside the db/data folder, I created two CSV files.

  • demo.valuehelp-Categories.csv
  • demo.valuehelp-Products.csv

The Categories CSV file contains the values that will be displayed in the Value Help dialog.

Below is the content of the Categories CSV file.

code,description
ELEC,Electronics
FURN,Furniture
BOOK,Books

The Products CSV file contains sample product records.

ID,name,category,price
3b5a1e2a-1111-4a1a-9a1a-000000000001,Laptop,ELEC,55000.00
3b5a1e2a-1111-4a1a-9a1a-000000000002,Office Chair,FURN,4500.00
3b5a1e2a-1111-4a1a-9a1a-000000000003,Notebook Set,BOOK,250.00

Step 3 – Create the Service

Next, create a service.cds file inside the srv folder.

In this file, I exposed both entities as projections so that they are available through OData services.

Below is the content of my service.cds file.

using demo.valuehelp as db from '../db/schema';

service CatalogService {
  entity Products   as projection on db.Products;
  entity Categories as projection on db.Categories;
}

Step 4 – Configure Value Help

After exposing the required entities in the service, I created a Fiori application inside the app folder by selecting New Project from Template in SAP Business Application Studio. I chose my local CAP application as the data source, and the project automatically generated the annotations.cds file.

In this file, I added the required UI annotations and configured the @Common.ValueList annotation for the Category field. The CollectionPath property points to the Categories entity, while LocalDataProperty and ValueListProperty map the selected value from the Value Help dialog back to the Product entity.

One thing I learned during this implementation is that even a small typo in an annotation or entity name can prevent the Value Help from working, while no clear error is shown in the UI. I found it helpful to verify the service metadata and ensure that all entity and property names matched exactly before troubleshooting further. This saved me a significant amount of debugging time.

Below is the content of my annotations.cds file.

using CatalogService as service from '../../srv/service';

annotate service.Products with @(
  UI.SelectionFields: [ category, name ],
  UI.LineItem: [
    { Value: name },
    { Value: category },
    { Value: price }
  ]
) {
  category @Common.ValueList : {
    Label          : 'Category',
    CollectionPath : 'Categories',
    Parameters     : [
      { $Type: 'Common.ValueListParameterInOut', LocalDataProperty: category, ValueListProperty: 'code' },
      { $Type: 'Common.ValueListParameterDisplayOnly', ValueListProperty: 'description' }
    ]
  };
};

Step 5 – Run the Application

After completing the above configuration, open the terminal and execute the following command.

cds watch

Once the application starts successfully, open the generated Fiori application.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 


here click on the URL which is having index.html
In this application I created value Help for a Selection field I mean a Search field.
Click On the Value Help Icon 
Here is the Image How My Value Help is looking

How Does It Work?

Let's quickly understand what happens behind the scenes.

  • The Categories entity stores all valid category values.
  • The Products entity contains the Category field.
  • The service.cds file exposes both entities.
  • The annotations.cds file connects the Product Category field with the Categories entity by using the @Common.ValueList annotation.
  • When the application runs, SAP Fiori automatically recognizes the annotation and displays the Value Help dialog.

Because of this configuration, users don't have to type category values manually, reducing data entry mistakes and improving consistency.

Conclusion

In this blog, we learned how to implement Value Help in SAP CAPM by using a lookup entity, service projections, and CDS annotations.

We created the required database entities, loaded sample data using CSV files, exposed the entities through a CAP service, and configured the @Common.ValueList annotation to display the Value Help dialog in the SAP Fiori application.

This approach is simple, reusable, and can be applied to many business scenarios such as Customers, Suppliers, Departments, Countries, Employees, or any other master data.

I hope this blog helps you understand how Value Help works in SAP CAPM.

Thank you for reading!

entry.title: The Fourth Way to Go Offline in UI5 (And Why the Service Worker Stays Dumb)
entry.content:
Written up after attending "Offline capabilities in UI5 – Let's discuss" at UI5Con, run by Viktor Sperling and Lars Kissel from SAP's UI5 team as an open discussion rather than a one-way talk. The app described below is a real, shipped project — a handheld scanning app for warehouse/inventory operations — with client-identifying details deliberately left out.

Three ideas, three shapes of "offline"

The session's core insight, stated up front, was that "offline" isn't one problem — it has a shape, and the right architecture depends on that shape. They presented three ideas, each solving a distinct scenario:

  • Idea 1 — OData V4 Model as Change Buffer. v4.ODataModel holds changes locally (updateGroupId), disables actions that can't succeed offline, submits once back online (submitBatch). Connectivity is actively probed — a fetch/AbortController ping, not just navigator.onLine. Scenario: a commuter on a train, signal drops between two stations. Duration: seconds to minutes.
  • Idea 2 — Service Worker as OData Proxy. The service worker becomes an OData-aware proxy: it parses $batch and read requests, resolves query options, keeps its own IndexedDB store and changelog, and rebuilds a combined $batch on reconnect. Scenario: a field worker visiting customers, connection comes and goes. Duration: minutes to hours.
  • Idea 3 — JSONModel with Explicit Sync. An explicit download → work → submit workflow. JSONModel as the UI model, a v4.ODataModel downloads the required entity sets up front, changes go into a changelog, submitted via Context#setProperty/#delete. Scenario: a traveler on a flight, working for hours in airplane mode. Duration: hours to days.

Idea 1 is elegant if you can stay inside the OData V4 model's own change-tracking. Idea 3 matches the shape of what we needed almost exactly — an explicit, user-initiated offline session, not an accidental one.

Idea 2 is the one worth pausing on, because it's easy to misread it as "just cache the OData responses." It doesn't. It builds its own IndexedDB store and changelog inside the service worker, and re-implements a slice of the OData protocol there — parsing $batch payloads, matching query options against locally held entity sets, replaying a changelog into a fresh $batch on reconnect. That's a genuinely more sophisticated design than an HTTP cache, and it's also exactly why it didn't fit our case: it means OData-protocol knowledge and business logic now live inside a service worker — a context that's already awkward to debug, awkward to unit test, and now also carries backend-shaped logic.

The session closed with a "Pain Points" slide worth quoting directly, because it names this tension independent of any particular implementation:

Back-end logic in the client — understanding the OData protocol, $filter options that quickly become complex, resolving navigation properties locally, duplicating business logic, OData Actions, server-side calculated fields.

Conflicts at sync time — concurrent modifications or business-rule violations, and expired authentication or session tokens, refresh before sync.

That first point is the crux of this whole post: the problem isn't "service workers shouldn't hold data." The problem is putting protocol-aware business logic inside a service worker specifically. The approach below solves the same problem idea 2 solves — resilience across a flaky connection — without paying that price.

Our shape of offline

Before the architecture, the shape of the problem, because it explains every choice below:

  • Runs on ruggedized handheld scanners with genuinely intermittent connectivity — sometimes hours without a signal, not a brief tunnel dip.
  • Scans in bulk via RFID — hundreds of local mutations in a single session before anything needs to reach a server.
  • Submits as a batch at the end of a session, not request-per-scan — the backend should never see a half-finished state.

That's squarely "spontaneous, business-logic-heavy, shared" territory — borrowing the session's own three-axis framing (Preparation: predictable ↔ spontaneous; Interaction complexity: simple form ↔ business logic; Conflict sensitivity: exclusive ↔ shared) — which rules out idea 1 immediately and makes idea 2's in-worker protocol logic feel like the wrong place to carry that weight.

A fourth option: two layers, two jobs

The architecture splits cleanly into two layers that don't know about each other's internals.

Two isolated layers on the device. The service worker never touches OData or $batch; the JSON model/IndexedDB layer owns all sync logic and only reaches the backend at two named moments — a fetch at the start of a session, a $batch submit at the end.

Layer 1 — the shell, and only the shell

The service worker knows nothing about OData, nothing about business entities. Its fetch strategy has three branches:

  • Skip — OData entity sets, lookup endpoints, $batch: always straight to the network, never cached. Stale business data is worse than a failed fetch, and offline resilience for that data lives entirely in the layer below.
  • Network-first with a timeout race — our own app resources (JS/CSS/XML/i18n): online gets fresh content plus a cache update, offline or a timeout falls back to cache. We deliberately avoid navigator.onLine — it lies in both directions. Racing a real fetch() against a timeout is symmetrically correct for "no network" and "server unreachable" alike.
  • Cache-first — third-party/library resources that rarely change.
const NETWORK_TIMEOUT_MS = 2000;

function rejectAfter(ms) {
  return new Promise((_, reject) =>
    setTimeout(() => reject(new Error("SW network-timeout")), ms)
  );
}

async function networkFirst(request) {
  const cache = await caches.open(CACHE_NAME);
  try {
    const response = await Promise.race([fetch(request), rejectAfter(NETWORK_TIMEOUT_MS)]);
    if (isCacheable(response)) cache.put(request, response.clone());
    return response;
  } catch {
    const cached = await cache.match(request, { ignoreSearch: true });
    if (cached) return cached;
    throw new Error("no network, no cache");
  }
}

One gotcha worth flagging for anyone building this themselves: cross-origin <script src> loads — like the UI5 runtime, loaded no-cors — come back as opaque responses. response.ok is false even on success, but the body is perfectly cacheable. Skip that check and your library bundles silently never get cached.

Loading UI5 itself deserves its own paragraph. Our bootstrap script picks its UI5 source depending on where it's running: an on-premise deployment loads sap-ui-core.js from the same server (no internet dependency at all), local dev loads from the public SAPUI5 CDN. "Offline-first" starts at the very first <script> tag — if you fetch the UI5 runtime itself from a CDN, your app is, by definition, not offline-capable anywhere without internet access. Those two sources produce different response types (same-origin vs. cross-origin no-cors), which is exactly why the opaque check above matters — without it, cache-first would silently never cache the UI5 runtime in the on-premise case.

Also worth knowing: the service worker doesn't register at all when the app runs inside a Fiori Launchpad — more on why below.

Layer 2 — data, entirely in ordinary application code

IndexedDB is the source of truth; the JSON model is loaded from it on boot and reloaded after every mutation. OData traffic isn't scattered through the app — it happens at exactly two named operations:

  1. Fetch — pull existing records from the backend when online, including a bulk status update so colleagues can see something's been picked up.
  2. Submit — at the end of a session, one $batch per document with everything — header update, item/serial changes — inside a single changeSet, so the backend never sees a half-written state.
private _queueHeaderUpdate(record: Order): void {
  this.model.update(getHeaderPath(record.id), {
    status: "3",
    processedBy: record.processedBy,
    processedAt: toBackendTimestamp()
  }, { merge: true, groupId: BATCH_GROUP, changeSetId: CHANGE_SET });
}

private _submitBatch(): Promise<void> {
  return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
    this.model.submitChanges({
      groupId: BATCH_GROUP,
      success: (data) => this._hasError(data) ? reject(data) : resolve(),
      error: reject
    });
  });
}

After a successful sync, the server's response is merged back into the local record — fields the server owns get overwritten, purely local UI state stays as-is.

This is, in workflow terms, closest to idea 3 — download, work, submit — but hardened with a persistent IndexedDB layer underneath instead of a purely in-memory JSONModel, so a page refresh mid-session doesn't throw everything away.

The bit I'm not proud to admit: session expiry

An architecture built around hours of offline work immediately raises a question: what happens to the session/XSRF token when you come back online? Honest answer: we don't have a good one yet.

  • There's no custom 401/403 detection or re-login flow anywhere in the app. Every OData call leans entirely on the OData V2 model's default XSRF fetch/retry behavior.
  • The batch-submit code only checks HTTP status codes (>= 400) to decide whether a sync succeeded — it doesn't distinguish "the server rejected this data" from "the session expired, retry after a token refresh."
  • Practical consequence: if a device has been offline for a day and the underlying session has since expired, the first sync batch after reconnecting fails as a generic error, not a recognizable "please log in again."

Reassurance, not excuse. This isn't a shortcoming unique to this approach. The session's own Pain Points slide names it directly: "expired authentication or session tokens — refresh before sync." Even SAP's own UI5 team presents this as an open problem across all three of their ideas, not something any of them has solved. That took some pressure off having to invent something clever here — naming it honestly is enough.

This isn't a showstopper in practice (logging back in on the device always fixes it), but it is a real gap: this architecture solves the transport problem, not the identity problem. "Offline-first" here does not mean "session-independent." You'd need to layer explicit 401 detection with a clear re-auth prompt on top if that matters for your case.

How the cache invalidates itself

Every build — production or local dev start — stamps a fresh build timestamp into a small generated file, imported into the service worker via importScripts. The service worker script itself never changes between builds; only that one value does.

self.BUILD_VERSION = "1737012345678"; // stamped per build

const CACHE_NAME = "app-v" + self.BUILD_VERSION;

self.addEventListener("activate", (event) => {
  event.waitUntil(
    caches.keys().then((keys) =>
      Promise.all(
        keys.filter((k) => k.startsWith("app-v") && k !== CACHE_NAME)
            .map((k) => caches.delete(k))
      )
    )
  );
});

No semver, no manual version bump to forget — a timestamp per build means the cache invalidates itself automatically on every build/start. Worth noting separately: the app's manifest version is a static marker, unrelated to this cache version or the OData service version — a deliberate decoupling, since it's tempting to conflate all three kinds of "version."

The Fiori Launchpad has its own appetite for the network

A natural question: could this run inside a Fiori Launchpad instead of standalone? In our case, no — and it's worth explaining why, because the reason generalizes.

A Launchpad is itself an application full of network-dependent shell services that have nothing to do with the app you're hosting inside it: personalization (tile layout, saved filters), intent/navigation resolution against a catalog, notifications, enterprise search, usage tracking, user preferences and theming. None of that is "your app" — you don't control it as an app builder, and none of it is designed to gracefully degrade without a network. That's exactly why registering a custom service worker under a Launchpad route conflicts with the shell: it already has its own lifecycle and caching assumptions, and a second service worker layered on top produces, at best, inconsistent behavior. Practically, our app runs standalone via a direct deep link, not as a Launchpad tile.

Speculative — a wish, not a feature. An officially supported "shell offline mode" would let an individual app register its own service worker scoped strictly to its own sub-path — the shell owning the root scope — and would let shell services degrade gracefully (personalization/notifications/search no-op instead of blocking) rather than all-or-nothing. To be clear, I'm not aware this exists today; it's the question this project left open, and a good one to put back to the community.

What about Fiori Elements and OData V4?

Also raised at the session: Fiori Elements with V4 bindings makes offline hard. I think that's right, and it's worth explaining why rather than just asserting it.

Fiori Elements is metadata- and annotation-driven: value helps, filters, side effects after an edit, and draft handling are, at their core, live interactions with the OData V4 service — they're not designed to be replayed from a local cache. sap.ui.model.odata.v4.ODataModel does keep an in-memory cache of already-loaded data, but that's request-level caching within a session, not a persistent offline store with conflict resolution the way a dedicated IndexedDB layer is. Historically, SAP's "real" offline-OData story has lived more at the native/mobile SDK level than in-browser Fiori Elements — worth double-checking the current state of that, since this area moves.

Practically, the options that hold up today, roughly in order of "how much Fiori Elements you keep":

  1. Limit offline to read-mostly reference data (cache value-help lists locally), keep write flows online.
  2. Freestyle islands inside an otherwise Fiori-Elements-driven app for exactly the screens that need to work offline.
  3. Go fully freestyle, as we did — you lose FE's automation (annotations → UI), but gain full control over what lives locally.

Fiori Elements optimizes for speed and consistency while you're online; the moment offline becomes a hard requirement, you're trading away precisely the automation that makes FE valuable — and freestyle starts looking attractive again.

Lessons that cost us a debugging session

  • Silent cache misses on lazy-loaded fragments. Dialogs that only load at runtime via Fragment.load failed silently if they weren't explicitly named in the pre-cache list — an empty dialog, no error — and the symptom only ever showed up under genuine offline testing, never in normal dev with a network.
  • navigator.onLine lies. It's true whenever a network interface exists, not when a server is reachable, and false isn't reliable either. A race between a real fetch() and a timeout is symmetrically correct in both directions.
  • Two UI5 sources, two response types. The same bootstrap code sometimes loads UI5 same-origin, sometimes cross-origin/no-cors — normal vs. opaque responses — and a cacheability check that only looks at response.ok silently misses the second case.
  • A service worker and a Fiori Launchpad shell don't mix. Registering a custom SW under a Launchpad route caused conflicts with the shell's own layer; registration had to be explicitly excluded for those routes.
  • Deliberately no IndexedDB schema migration. On a breaking model change, we chose a new database name over a migration path — old local data is simply ignored, not converted. For a small, known fleet of devices carrying mostly in-transit data, that's a cheaper, safer call than maintaining migration code.
  • Session expiry isn't solved for free by any of this — see above. Worth saying explicitly so the takeaway isn't "offline-first means never thinking about sessions again."

When this pattern fits — and when it doesn't

This isn't "the best option" — it's the option that fits a specific shape of app. The session's own "Technical Aspects" slide offers a genuinely useful way to reason about that shape: three axes — Preparation (predictable ↔ spontaneous), Interaction complexity (simple form ↔ business logic), and Conflict sensitivity (exclusive ↔ shared). Our app sits firmly at the right-hand end of every axis — unpredictable offline moments, non-trivial document logic, multiple colleagues touching the same stock. That's exactly why idea 1, built for predictable and simple cases, wasn't a fit.

Fits well:

  • Apps where users mutate a lot before anything needs to reach a server (scan sessions, inspections, field forms) — the win is decoupling "mutating" from "submitting."
  • Longer, unpredictable offline periods (minutes to hours), not just a brief tunnel dip.
  • Business logic that doesn't fit neatly into a generic entity cache — merges, batch/changeSet semantics, status derived from other fields, locally created records that only get a server ID at sync time.
  • A limited, known set of devices/users, where "no automatic IndexedDB migration" and "manual session recovery" are acceptable trade-offs.

Fits less well:

  • Apps where nearly every action needs a direct server round-trip anyway (real-time collaborative, prices/stock that must be correct right now).
  • Short, second-scale, unprepared offline blips: idea 1 is probably cheaper to build and maintain — you get most of the resilience for free from the model itself.
  • Many concurrent users editing the same records ("shared" on the conflict-sensitivity axis): this pattern solves "one device, local, sync later," not multi-user conflict resolution. Look at CRDTs, optimistic locking with real conflict UI, or just staying online with a good loading state instead.
  • Teams that would rather lean on a standardized offline framework than maintain a custom SW + IndexedDB layer.

A short aside: why not Cordova, Capacitor, or a native SDK?

A fair question: why a PWA/service-worker approach, and not a hybrid or native app that's offline-capable by default? First, a framing correction: a service worker isn't an experimental bet — it's a mature, broadly supported web standard. This isn't "proven vs. experimental," it's "proven vs. proven."

  • Apache Cordova (formerly PhoneGap) wraps a web app in a native webview shell with plugin access to device APIs. Still an active Apache project, but development velocity has been low for years — much of the hybrid ecosystem has moved toward Capacitor. Worth checking current release cadence and plugin health before repeating that as fact, since it dates quickly.
  • Capacitor (Ionic) is the de facto successor for "web app in a native shell" — a similar model, more actively maintained, a more modern plugin architecture.
  • Native, vendor-specific offline SDKs often ship a ready-made offline layer with built-in conflict resolution — at the cost of vendor lock-in: a proprietary data model/SDK, lower portability, and the loss of "it's just a web app, patch with a new build."

We stayed on web/PWA for three reasons: it's built on proven, stable technology rather than a moving target; a new build is a set of static files picked up by the service worker on the next online load — no app-store review, no MDM rollout cycle per patch; and the sync logic is our own readable code against a standard OData service, not a proprietary SDK locking us to one vendor.

Where this leaves things

None of this is a silver bullet, and it isn't meant to replace the session's three ideas — it's meant to sit alongside them as a fourth shape for a fourth kind of problem. If your offline story is short and unprepared, idea 1 will get you further with less code. If you want the app itself to stay entirely unaware of offline handling, idea 2's service-worker-as-proxy is a genuinely clever answer, provided you're comfortable carrying protocol logic in that layer. If your offline sessions are long, deliberate, and business-logic-heavy — ours were — this is the shape that held up.

Thanks to Viktor and Lars for running the session as a discussion instead of a monologue — it's the reason this idea exists in writable form at all. Curious how others have handled the session-expiry gap in particular — that felt like the one open thread nobody in the room had a clean answer for.

entry.title: Advanced VizFrame Customization in SAPUI5 OVP Applications
entry.content:
Advanced VizFrame Customization in SAPUI5 OVP Applications

Introduction

As we all know, that we can create Fiori charts easily using the standard templates provided by SAP, but it does not give enough flexibility to customize on top of that, when we want to make any changes in those standard templates then we must make use of custom charts. For custom charts, VizFrames are used to perform the customization on the chart as per the requirement.

In one of my recent implementations, I defined the custom chart using VizFrame with two lines and two columns on x and y axis. Apart from this there was a dynamic colour change option given to the user, so that they can dynamically change colors of both the lines and columns, and the colour once picked, has persisted in the localStorage of the browser for next time use.

This blog explains the implementation steps:

Business Requirement

  • Dual combination chart (2lines and 2 bars)
  • Allow user to personalize chart colours.
  • Saves the colour preference.

Solution

To achieve the above requirement, we have used

  • sap.viz.ui5.controls.VizFrame
  • dual_combinatoin chart type
  • ColorPickerPopover
  • Sap.m.Menu
  • Browser localStorage

Step1: Create a dual combination chart using VizFrame:

The custom chart is created using VizFrame with dual_combination as the chart type.

<viz:VizFrame id="idVizFrame" vizType="dual_combination" uiConfig="{applicationSet:'fiori'}">
</viz:VizFrame>

Step2: Define the chart dimensions and measures:

Here, I am defining the chart dimensions and measures.

Dimension:<vizDataset:DimensionDefinition
                             name="Period"
                             value="{Date}" />

Measure:  <vizDataset:MeasureDefinition
                             name="Metric A"
                              value="{Value1}" />

                     <vizDataset:MeasureDefinition
                             name="Metric B"
                             value="{Value2}" />

                   <vizDataset:MeasureDefinition
                             name="Metric C"
                             value="{Value3}" />

                 <vizDataset:MeasureDefinition
                             name="Metric D"
                             value="{Value4}" />

Step3: Configuring chart feeds:

First, I have configured the primary axis to display column measures and then the secondary axis to display line measures.

<vizFeeds:FeedItem uid="valueAxis" type="Measure" values="Metric A,Metric B"/>

<vizFeeds:FeedItem uid="valueAxis2" type="Measure" values="Metric C,Metric D"/>

<vizFeeds:FeedItem uid="categoryAxis" type="Dimension" values="Period"/>

Step4: Creating colour palette button:

Enabling palette button as drop-down menu, so that the user can select the measure for which personalized colour needs to be picked.

<Button
       icon="sap-icon://palette"
       type="Transparent"
       tooltip="Change Chart Colors"
       press=".onOpenColorSettings"/>

When the user clicks on the menu item, a color selection menu appears.

onOpenColorSettings: function(oEvent) {
        if (!this._oColorMenu) {
            this._oColorMenu = new sap.m.Menu({
                items: [
                   new sap.m.MenuItem({ 
                   text: "Metric A",
                   press: this.onMeasureSelect.bind(this, "Metric A")   }),
                   new sap.m.MenuItem({
                   text: "Metric B",
                   press: this.onMeasureSelect.bind(this, "Metric B")  })
               ]
         });
      }
     this.oColorMenu.openBy(oEvent.getSource());
    }

As shown in the snap below, we get a colour palette button, with the dropdown menu and all the required options, and when we select on each one, we get the colour picker as shown in the snap below.

     

Step5: Define the default colours during Initialization:

I have set the default colour values for all measures inside onInit() method.

this.aColors = {
     "Metric A": "#2E86AB",
     "Metric B": "#28B463",
     "Metric C": "#F39C12",
     "Metric D": "#8E44AD"
 };

Step6: Applying personalized colours using dataPointStyle in VizFrame:

I have used VizFrame's dataPointStyle config to apply personalized colours to the measures.

plotArea: {
     dataPointStyle: {
          rules: [
            { 
              dataContext: { measureNames: "Metric A"},
               properties: {color: "#2E86AB"}
            },
           {
               dataContext: {measureNames: "Metric B"},
               properties: {color: "#28B463"}
           }
         ]

Step7: Open color picker when the measure is selected:

When the measure is selected from the menu, a color picker dialog appears.

_openColorPicker: function(oControl, sMeasure) {

        if (!this._oColorPicker) {
             this._oColorPicker = new sap.ui.unified.ColorPickerPopover({
                  colorString: "HEX",
                  change: fuction(oEvent) {
                       var sColor = oEvent.getParameter("colorString");
                        this.applyMeasureColor( sMeasure, sColor );
                  }.bind(this)
             });
           }
       this._oColorPicker.openBy(oControl);
      }

Step8: To dynamically update the VizFrame properties:

The selected color is updated dynamically by updating the VizFrame properties.

_applyMeasureColor: function(sMeasure, sColor) {
          var oVizFrame = this.byId("idVizFrame");
          var oProps = oVizFrame.getVizProperties();
          oProps.plotArea.dataPointStyle.rules =
                oProps.plotArea.dataPointStyle.rules.map(function(oRule) {
                      if (oRule.dataContext.measureNames === sMeasure) {
                               oRule.properties.color = sColor;
                        }
                   return oRule;
                });
               oVizFrame.setVizProperties(oProps);
    }

Step9: Persist the selected colours using browser local storage:

The user selected colors are stored in the browser's local storage, so that the preferred colors are picked up when you reopen the application.

localStorage.setItem(
        "CHART_COLORS",
          JSON.stringify(oColorObject)
    );

We can retrieve the same during initialization, so that the colours remain consistent even while reopening the application.
 

 JSON.parse(
        localStorage.getItem("CHART_COLORS")
   );

Output

Here is the final working chart snap:

Conclusion

This shows that instead of limiting to pre-defined charts or templates, one can personalize the charts and visualize their own preferences and in turn makes it easy to read and analyse data using VizFrame.

Reference: Documentation - Demo Kit - SAPUI5 SDK

entry.title: Build an Auto-Rotating Image Slider in SAPUI5 using Carousel Control.
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

Introduction  

While working on one of my SAPUI5 applications, I wanted the landing page to look more attractive instead of displaying a static image.  

After exploring different approaches, I found that combining the Carousel control with JavaScript's setInterval() function provides a simple and effective solution. In this blog, I'll share how I implemented an auto-rotating image slider and explain the logic behind it so that you can use the same approach in your SAPUI5 applications.  

The sap.m.Carousel control is a container control in SAPUI5 that allows users to navigate through a collection of content items. Each item within the Carousel can be an image, form, layout, or any other SAPUI5 control.  

Overview  

The Carousel control is commonly used for displaying image galleries, but it can also contain any sap.m control.  

Structure  

The carousel consists of the following elements:  

  • Content area - displays the different items.  
  • Navigation - arrows to the left and right for switching between items.  
  • Paging (optional) - indicator at the bottom to show the current position in the set.  

The paging indicator behaves as follows:  

  • showPageIndicator: Determines whether the indicator is displayed.  
  • If the dataset contains less than 9 pages, the indicator displays as bullets.  
  • If the dataset contains 9 or more pages, the indicator becomes numeric.  
  • pageIndicatorPlacement: Determines where the indicator is located. The default setting is sap.m.CarouselPageIndicatorPlacementType.Bottom (below the content).  

Step 1 :   

Create the SAPUI5 Project Using a Basic Template  

Step 2 :  

Configure the XML View (View1.view.xml)  

In this view:  

  • The left side contains the image slider using the Carousel control.   
  • The right side contains Login and Sign Up forms using IconTabBar.   

I chose this layout because it resembles a common login page design used in many business applications. Although the focus of this blog is the Carousel, placing it beside a login form demonstrates a practical use case. 

<mvc:View controllerName="carouselcontrolproject.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m" 
    xmlns:form="sap.ui.layout.form">
    <Page id="page" title="{i18n>title}">
        <HBox width="100%" justifyContent="SpaceBetween">
            <Carousel id="id1" loop="true" width="600px" height="500px">
                <Image src="https://images.pexels.com/photos/16767121/pexels-photo-16767121.jpeg" />
                <Image src="https://images.pexels.com/photos/32162192/pexels-photo-32162192.jpeg" />
                <Image src="https://images.pexels.com/photos/32228687/pexels-photo-32228687.jpeg" />
                <Image src="https://images.pexels.com/photos/32730210/pexels-photo-32730210.jpeg" />
            </Carousel>
            <VBox width="50%">
                <IconTabBar>
                    <items>
                        <IconTabFilter text="Login">
                            <form:SimpleForm editable="true" layout="ResponsiveGridLayout">
                                <form:content>
                                    <Label text="Email" />
                                    <Input id="inputLEmail" value="" valueState="None" valueStateText="" liveChange="onLiveChangeLEmail" />
                                    
                                    <Label text="Password" />
                                    <Input id="inputLPassword" value="" valueState="None" valueStateText="" type="Password" liveChange="onLiveChangeLPass" />
                                    
                                    <Label />
                                    <VBox alignItems="Center">
                                        <Button text="Log in" type="Emphasized" press="onPressLogin" />
                                    </VBox>
                                    
                                    <Label />
                                    <Link text="Forgot Password?" press="onPressOpenForPassFrag" />
                                </form:content>
                            </form:SimpleForm>
                        </IconTabFilter>
                        <IconTabFilter text="Sign up">
                            <form:SimpleForm editable="true" layout="ResponsiveGridLayout">
                                <form:content>
                                    <Label text="Name" />
                                    <Input id="inputSName" value="" valueStateText="" liveChange="onLiveChangeName" />
                                    
                                    <Label text="Email" />
                                    <Input id="inputSEmail" value="" valueStateText="" liveChange="onLiveChangeEmail" />
                                    
                                    <Label text="Password" />
                                    <Input id="inputSPassword" type="Password" value="" valueStateText="" liveChange="onLiveChangePass" />
                                    
                                    <Label text="Confirm Password" />
                                    <Input id="inputCPassword" type="Password" value="" valueStateText="" />
                                    
                                    <Label />
                                    <HBox justifyContent="SpaceBetween">
                                        <Button text="Reset" type="Emphasized" press="resetSFields" />
                                        <Button text="Sign Up" type="Emphasized" press="onPressSignUp" />
                                    </HBox>
                                </form:content>
                            </form:SimpleForm>
                        </IconTabFilter>
                    </items>
                </IconTabBar>
            </VBox>
        </HBox>
    </Page>
</mvc:View>

In my example, I configured the Carousel with loop="true" so that the images continue rotating without interruption. I also assigned a fixed width and height to maintain a consistent layout beside the login form. Each Image control represents one slide in the Carousel. 

Note: Since external image URLs are used from Pexels, an active internet connection is required to load the images. 

Step 3: 

Implement Auto-Rotation Logic (View1.controller.js) 

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller"
], function (Controller) {
    "use strict";

    return Controller.extend("carouselcontrolproject.controller.View1", {
        onInit: function () {
            // Fetch the Carousel control instance
            var oCarousel = this.byId("id1");
            
            // Set up a safe interval function for auto-rotation
            this._iIntervalId = setInterval(function () {
                if (oCarousel) {
                    oCarousel.next();
                }
            }, 1000);
        },

        onExit: function () {
            // Clean up the interval when the view is destroyed to prevent memory leaks
            if (this._iIntervalId) {
                clearInterval(this._iIntervalId);
            }
        }
    });
});

I used JavaScript's setInterval() function because it provides a simple way to execute the same action repeatedly. Every second, it calls the Carousel's next() method, allowing the images to rotate automatically without any user interaction. 

Note : For demonstration purposes, the interval is set to 1 second. In production applications, a value between 3 and 5 seconds generally provides a better user experience. 

Step-by-Step Execution 

  1. View loads  
  2. onInit() executes automatically  
  3. Carousel with ID id1 is fetched  
  4. The setInterval() callback function executes.  
  5. The next() method is called on the Carousel.  
  6. The Carousel navigates to the next image.  
  7. If the last image is reached and loop="true" is enabled, navigation continues from the first image. 

Output 

The application displays: 

  • An auto-rotating image slider on the left side  
  • Login and Sign Up forms on the right side  
  • Images automatically change every second using JavaScript interval logic 

Conclusion 

While implementing this feature, I realized that SAPUI5 already provides everything needed to build a simple image slider without relying on external UI libraries. By combining the standard sap.m.Carousel control with a small amount of JavaScript, I was able to create an auto-rotating banner that can easily be reused in landing pages, dashboards, or product showcase applications.

I hope this example helps anyone looking to add a more dynamic and engaging user interface to their SAPUI5 applications.

entry.title: UI5 Linter in Action: A Hands-On Workshop at UI5con 2026
entry.content:
UI5 Linter in Action: A Hands-On Workshop at UI5con 2026

Still running legacy OpenUI5/SAPUI5 code with deprecated APIs, inline scripts, and global variables? It's time to modernize — and this workshop shows you exactly how.

What Happened

At UI5con 2026 Germany, we ran a one-hour hands-on workshop where attendees took a real OpenUI5/SAPUI5 application from "legacy" to "future-proof" using UI5 linter. Step by step, participants:

  • Ran their first UI5 linter report and learned what it tells them
  • Auto-fixed issues with a single command
  • Manually resolved deprecated APIs, CSP violations, and legacy patterns
  • Integrated UI5 linter into a CI pipeline so regressions never come back

The feedback was fantastic — attendees loved the practical, hands-on format and walked away with real skills they could immediately apply to their own projects.

Do It Yourself

Couldn't make it to UI5con? No problem. This workshop is fully self-paced and available for you to complete on your own. All you need:

Prerequisites

  • Node.js (LTS) and Git installed
  • A code editor of your choice
  • Basic UI5 development experience

Simply clone the workshop repository and follow the step-by-step instructions. You'll get the same experience as the live attendees — modernizing a real app from start to finish at your own pace.

What You'll Learn

  • How the UI5 linter identifies deprecated APIs, CSP violations, and legacy patterns
  • How to auto-fix common issues with a single command
  • How to manually resolve findings that require deeper changes
  • How to integrate UI5 linter into your CI pipeline for ongoing code quality

Bonus: AI-Assisted Modernization

As an outlook, the workshop briefly explores the UI5 Modernization plugin for coding agents — automating the very steps you'll learn manually.

Clone the repo, follow the steps, and modernize your first app today!

entry.title: Visualizing Business Processes in SAPUI5 using the sap.suite.ui.commons.ProcessFlow Control
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

In this blog post I'm going to explain the ProcessFlow control in SAPUI5 in a simple way, with the steps I followed while trying it out.

Overview:

So, in simple words, ProcessFlow is a control which is used to show a chain of connected documents as one single picture, instead of showing them as separate rows in a table.

For example, in most SAP scenarios one document leads to another document. A Sales Order leads to a Delivery, and the Delivery leads to an Invoice. If we show these in a normal list, all three will appear as separate rows, and the user cannot easily tell how they are connected or which one is stuck. So, by using the ProcessFlow control, we can show all three documents together in one single flow, with colors showing the status of each document.

So here I have created one small project to try this out, where I have taken a simple Order-to-Cash example - Sales Order, Delivery, and Invoice - and shown them using the ProcessFlow control.

Why I used ProcessFlow instead of building it myself

So, first I thought of building this on my own using some divs and CSS and manually giving colors based on status. But then I realized SAPUI5 already gives a standard control for this, so I did not have to build the status colors, click behaviour, or zoom behaviour on my own. Also, since it is a standard Fiori control, it looks and behaves the same as other Fiori apps, so I did not have to do any extra styling.

Project Structure 

The important files for this control are inside the webapp folder, mainly the view, controller, model, and manifest.json file, same as any normal SAPUI5 app.

processflowcontrol/ 
├── webapp/ 
│   ├── controller/ 
│   │   ├── App.controller.js 
│   │   └── View1.controller.js 
│   ├── css/ 
│   ├── i18n/ 
│   ├── model/ 
│   │   ├── data.json  
│   │   └── models.js 
│   ├── test/ 
│   ├── view/ 
│   │   ├── App.view.xml 
│   │   └── View1.view.xml 
│   ├── Component.js 
│   ├── index.html 
│   └── manifest.json 
├── package.json 
└── README.md

Step 1: Declaring the Data Model in manifest.json 

So, first we have to declare our JSON model in the manifest.json file, so that the framework will load it automatically when the app starts. We don't need to write any extra code in the controller for this.

"models": { 
 "i18n": { 
   "type": "sap.ui.model.resource.ResourceModel", 
   "settings": { 
     "bundleName": "processflowcontrol.i18n.i18n" 
   } 
 }, 
 "process": { 
   "type": "sap.ui.model.json.JSONModel", 
   "uri": "model/data.json" 
 } 
} 

Step 2: Create the JSON data (model/data.json)

So, the ProcessFlow control needs two things in the JSON file - lanes and nodes.

lanes means the stages, like Sales Order, Delivery, and Invoice. nodes means the actual documents which sit inside these stages.

{ 
 "lanes": [ 
   { "id": "order",    "label": "Sales Order", "icon": "sap-icon://sales-order",     "position": 0 }, 
   { "id": "delivery", "label": "Delivery",     "icon": "sap-icon://shipping-status", "position": 1 }, 
   { "id": "invoice",  "label": "Invoice",      "icon": "sap-icon://receipt",         "position": 2 } 
 ], 
 "nodes": [ 
   { "id": "SO-1001",  "lane": "order",    "title": "SO 1001",       "abbr": "SO", "state": "Positive", "stateText": "Completed", "children": ["DEL-2001"] }, 
   { "id": "DEL-2001", "lane": "delivery", "title": "Delivery 2001", "abbr": "DE", "state": "Positive", "stateText": "Shipped",   "children": ["INV-3001"] }, 
   { "id": "INV-3001", "lane": "invoice",  "title": "Invoice 3001",  "abbr": "IN", "state": "Critical", "stateText": "Overdue",   "children": null } 
 ] 
}

 

A few important points about the JSON data:

  • position (on lanes) — controls the left-to-right order of the lanes. I removed it once in my own testing file just to check, and the Delivery lane came before the Sales Order lane. So this field decides the order, and it's easy to forget.

  • children (on nodes) — this is what draws the arrow to the next node. SO-1001 has DEL-2001 as its child, so an arrow is drawn from SO-1001 to DEL-2001, and so on. Since children is written as an array, we can give more than one document ID here if the process branches into two paths.

  • state (on nodes) — decides the color of the node:

    • Positive → green

    • Critical → orange

    • Negative → red

    • Neutral → grey

  • stateText (on nodes) — just the small label shown under the node (like "Completed" or "Overdue").

Step 3: Bind the control in the view (View1.view.xml)

<mvc:View controllerName="processflowcontrol.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m"
    xmlns:n="sap.suite.ui.commons">
    <Page id="page" title="{i18n>title}">
        <Panel headerText="Order Fulfillment Status">
            <n:ProcessFlow id="orderProcessFlow" scrollable="false" nodes="{process>/nodes}" lanes="{process>/lanes}" nodePress="onNodePressed">
                <n:nodes>
                    <n:ProcessFlowNode laneId="{process>lane}" nodeId="{process>id}" title="{process>title}" titleAbbreviation="{process>abbr}" children="{process>children}" state="{process>state}" stateText="{process>stateText}" highlighted="{process>highlighted}" />
                </n:nodes>
                <n:lanes>
                    <n:ProcessFlowLaneHeader laneId="{process>id}" text="{process>label}" iconsrc="{process>icon}" position="{process>position}" />
                </n:lanes>
            </n:ProcessFlow>
        </Panel>
    </Page>
</mvc:View>

So here we are simply binding the nodes and lanes arrays from our JSON model to the ProcessFlow control, and inside that giving the aggregation binding for ProcessFlowNode and ProcessFlowLaneHeader.

Step 4: Handling Node Clicks (View1.controller.js) 

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller",
    "sap/m/MessageToast"
], (Controller, MessageToast) => {
    "use strict";
    return Controller.extend("processflowcontrol.controller.View1", {
        onInit() {
        },
        onNodePressed: function (oEvent) {
            var oNode = oEvent.getSource();
            var sTitle = oNode.getTitle();
            MessageToast.show("Node selected: " + sTitle);
        }
    });
});

So, in the above code, whenever a user clicks on any node, the onNodePressed function will be called, and we get the clicked node using oEvent.getSource(). From there we can read its title, and any other property we need. I was a little confused initially whether to read the node details from getSource() or from the event parameters, but getSource() gave me the correct node object, so I used that.

Here I have just shown a MessageToast with the node title when clicked, but in an actual application, this is the place where we can navigate to the object page of that particular document.

Output

Conclusion:

So, this is how we can use the ProcessFlow control in SAPUI5 to show a chain of connected documents in a single, easy-to-understand picture. Instead of showing Sales Order, Delivery, and Invoice as separate rows in a table, we can show them together as one flow, with colors telling us the status of each document at a glance.We just need to give the control two things - lanes (the stages) and nodes (the documents) - in a simple JSON format, and the control takes care of the rest: drawing the lanes in order, placing the nodes correctly, drawing the arrows between them, coloring them based on status, and also giving us zoom and click functionality without any extra code.This same JSON structure is not limited to Sales Order-Delivery-Invoice - we can reuse it for any multi-step process, like a support ticket lifecycle, a purchase approval chain, or an onboarding process, just by changing the lane and node data.So this is a simple and quick way to build a document flow screen without writing our own timeline UI from scratch.

entry.title: Creating a Custom Action to Export Selected Records to Excel Using Fiori Elements and CAP
entry.content:
Introduction

While working on a Fiori Elements application, I came across a requirement that initially seemed straightforward but wasn't available as a standard feature. The application already had the built-in Export to Excel option, but it always exported every row displayed in the table. The business users wanted something slightly different—they wanted to select only a few records and export just those.

My first thought was to implement a backend action that generated an Excel file and returned it to the UI. After exploring the available options, I realized that wasn't necessary. SAPUI5 already provides a Spreadsheet API that can generate Excel files directly in the browser, making the implementation much simpler while avoiding additional backend development.

In this blog, I'll walk through the approach I used to add a custom Export Selected button to a Fiori Elements List Report. By the end, you'll have a working example that exports only the selected rows and integrates naturally with the standard Fiori toolbar.

Step 1: Initialize the CAP Project

First, create a new directory for your project and initialize a standard SAP CAP application. Open your terminal and run:

mkdir employee
cd employee
cds init

Step 2: Define the Database Schema

Next, we will define the data model for our employees.

Create a file named schema.cds inside the db folder and add the following code:

// db/schema.cds
namespace company.hr;
 
entity Employees {
    key ID     : UUID;
    name       : String;
    department : String;
    role       : String;
    email      : String;
}

Step 3: Create the Service and UI Annotations

Now, expose the database entity as an OData service and add the basic Fiori UI annotations to define what columns will appear in our Fiori List Report table.

Create a file named cat-services.cds (typically in the srv folder) and add this code:

// srv/cat-services.cds
using { company.hr as db } from '../db/schema';
 
service EmployeeService {
    entity Employees as projection on db.Employees;
}
 
annotate EmployeeService.Employees with @(
    UI.LineItem : [
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Employee Name', Value : name },
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Department', Value : department },
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Role', Value : role },
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Email', Value : email }
    ]
);

Step 4: Add Mock Data

To test our application, we need some dummy data. SAP CAP automatically loads CSV files that match the namespace and entity.

Create a folder named data inside your db folder, then create a file named company.hr-Employees.csv and paste the following:

ID;name;department;role;email
1;Alice Walker;Engineering;Backend Developer;alice@company.com
2;Bob Smith;Sales;Account Executive;bob@company.com
3;Charlie Davis;Engineering;Frontend Developer;charlie@company.com
4;Diana Prince;HR;HR Manager;diana@company.com

Step 5: Configure the Custom Action in the Page Map

Once your Fiori Elements application has been generated, the next step is to add a custom action to the table toolbar.

Follow these steps:

  1. Right-click on the webapp folder in your project.
  2. Select Show Page Map.
  3. In the Page Map, select the List Report page.
  4. Click the Edit (pencil) icon to modify the page.
  5. Under the Table / Table Toolbar section, click the + button next to Actions.
  6. Select Add Custom Action.

Configure the custom action with the following properties:

  • Label: Export Selected
  • Requires Selection: Enabled
  • Press Event: exportSelected

Step 6: Implement the Custom UI5 Export Logic

Now, we will write the logic that extracts the selected rows and converts them to an Excel file using the Fiori V4 API.

Open the newly generated controller extension file (ListReportExt.js) and replace its contents with the following:

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/export/Spreadsheet",
    "sap/m/MessageToast"
], function (Spreadsheet, MessageToast) {
    "use strict";
 
    return {
        exportSelected: function (oEvent) {
            
            // 1. Access the Fiori V4 API
            var oExtensionAPI = this.editFlow ? this : this.getExtensionAPI();
            
            // 2. Get only the rows selected by the user
            var aSelection = oExtensionAPI.getSelectedContexts();
 
            if (!aSelection || aSelection.length === 0) {
                MessageToast.show("Please select at least one employee to export.");
                return;
            }
 
            // 3. Extract the raw JSON data from those selected rows
            var aData = aSelection.map(function (oContext) {
                return oContext.getObject();
            });
 
            // 4. Map the Excel columns to your CDS database fields
            var aColumns = [
                { label: 'Employee Name', property: 'name' },
                { label: 'Department', property: 'department' },
                { label: 'Role', property: 'role' },
                { label: 'Email', property: 'email' }
            ];
 
            // 5. Configure the Excel Workbook properties
            var oSettings = {
                workbook: { 
                    columns: aColumns,
                    context: {
                        sheetName: 'Selected Employees'
                    }
                },
                dataSource: aData,
                fileName: "Custom_Employee_Export.xlsx",
                worker: false 
            };
 
            // 6. Build and Download the Excel file
            var oSheet = new Spreadsheet(oSettings);
            
            oSheet.build().then(function() {
                MessageToast.show("Excel export successful!");
            }).catch(function(sMessage) {
                MessageToast.show("Export failed: " + sMessage);
            }).finally(function () {
                oSheet.destroy(); // Destroy object to prevent memory leaks in the browser
            });
        }
    };
});

Step 7: Run and Test the Application

With the code in place, start your server by running:

cds watch

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

After running the application, select one or more employee records from the table. You'll notice that the Export Selected button is enabled only when records are selected. Clicking the button downloads an Excel file containing only the selected employees instead of the entire table.

 

 

 

 

Conclusion

What I liked most about this approach is that it keeps the implementation lightweight. Since the Excel file is generated in the browser using the SAPUI5 Spreadsheet library, there was no need to create a custom CAP action or write backend logic just for exporting data.

One thing I found useful was enabling Requires Selection for the custom action. Without it, users could click the button even when no rows were selected, resulting in unnecessary validation. Letting Fiori handle the button state provides a much better user experience.

Although this example exports employee data, the same approach can be used in almost any Fiori Elements application, such as Sales Orders, Purchase Orders, Expense Reports, or Products. If you've ever wanted to provide users with more control over what they export, this is a simple customization that's worth adding to your applications.

entry.title: JSX Runtime for UI5: write your views as TSX, and tell us what you think
entry.content:
JSX Runtime for UI5: write your views as TSX, and tell us what you think

We've been experimenting with something and we'd like to put it in front of the community early: a JSX runtime for UI5. It lets you author UI5 views as .tsx files, with full per-control TypeScript typing, and it does so without a virtual DOM, and without a reconciler.

This is an incubation. It works, the showcase runs end-to-end, but the whole point of this post is to get it into your hands and hear back from you before we commit to more. If you write UI5 apps, we want your opinion.

What it actually does

A JSX element compiles to a plain constructor call. That's the entire idea:

import Button from "sap/m/Button";
import Page from "sap/m/Page";

export default class Main extends View {
    createContent() {
        return (
            <Page title="Hello">
                <Button text="Click me" press=".onPress" />
            </Page>
        );
    }
}

<Button text="Click me" /> becomes new Button({ text: "Click me" }). Nothing more magical than that. Babel emits the calls, the runtime hands them to UI5, and from there it's UI5 exactly as you already know it: metadata, applySettings, bindings, aggregations, events. There's no re-render loop, because a JSX expression is a constructor call, not a render description. Reactivity comes from UI5 bindings, same as in an XML view.

One thing to set expectations up front: this JSX describes the UI5 control tree, not the DOM. There's no native HTML. A <div> or <span> won't work, because a lowercase tag can't become a UI5 control. You compose controls (<Page>, <Button>, <VBox>, …), exactly as you do in an XML view, and UI5's renderers produce the actual markup. (An HTML-aware renderer is on the radar, but it's not here today.)

If you don't use TypeScript, plain .jsx works too. You still get the new Control(settings) calls, you just skip the author-time type checking.

Why we think it's worth a look

  • Per-control typing that we didn't hand-write. <Button> is typed from Button's own $ButtonSettings interface, which @openui5/types already publishes from each control's metadata. Wrong prop names, wrong value types, wrong event payloads. The TypeScript checker catches them. No code generation step in the project.
  • It's the control tree you already have. XML views and TSX views coexist in the same app. You can migrate one view at a time and keep everything runnable, or never migrate at all and just write new views in TSX.
  • No fork of UI5. The runtime doesn't patch or replace anything in the core. It glues Babel's output into UI5's existing settings model.
  • A small set of directives that read well. <Fragment> for grouping, <For> for bound aggregations, <If> for conditional rendering and falsy children ({flag && <X/>}) just work without crashing an aggregation.
  • A plugin SPI, if you want to extend it. Five extension points let libraries add their own directives and behaviors on top of the core without touching it. Everything the core itself does, the dot-handler events, <For>, <If>, is built on that same SPI, so nothing in the core is privileged. We ship a <Switch>/<Case>/<Default> sample plugin as a worked example.

Where to start

The fastest way to get a feel for it is the live showcase. It's a running UI5 app that doubles as the documentation, with runnable samples for every concept:

👉https://ui5-community.github.io/jsx-runtime/

The source, the setup guide, and the monorepo (the library, the showcase, and a minimal from-scratch reference app) are on GitHub:

👉https://github.com/ui5-community/jsx-runtime

If you already have an app with XML views, there's a step-by-step guide for converting them one at a time.

This is where you come in

We're calling this an incubation on purpose. Before we invest further, we want to know whether it solves a real problem for you, where it feels awkward, and what's missing. Concretely, we'd love to hear:

  • Would you write new views in TSX? Would you migrate existing ones?
  • Does the typing carry its weight, or is it more ceremony than value?
  • What breaks, or what did you expect to work and it didn't?
  • What would you need before you'd use this in a real project?

Please open an issue with anything: a bug, a rough edge, a "this is great," a "this is not for me and here's why":

👉https://github.com/ui5-community/jsx-runtime/issues

Honest feedback, including the critical kind, is exactly what makes an incubation worth doing. Thanks for taking a look.

entry.title: Export Selected Rows to Excel in SAP Fiori Elements Using Custom Actions
entry.content:
Hello Everyone, 

While working on a CAP-based SAP Fiori Elements application, I came across a requirement where users needed to export only the selected records from the List Report page instead of the entire dataset. 

SAP Fiori Elements provides a standard Export option that downloads all the records displayed in the table. However, there may be business scenarios where users need to export only specific rows that they have selected. 

In this blog, I'll show you how to create a custom action that exports only the selected rows to an Excel file using the sap.ui.export.Spreadsheet API. 

Prerequisites 

Before implementing this solution, ensure that: 

  • You have a CAP-based SAP Fiori Elements application.  
  • Your List Report page is already displaying data.  
  • You are using SAPUI5 version that supports sap.ui.export.Spreadsheet.  
  • You have created a Controller Extension for your List Report. 

Step 1: Create a Custom Action Using a Controller Extension 

Add a handler function in the controller extension and update the manifest.json file to register the custom action. 

In this step, you create a controller extension to define a handler function for the custom action button and add the extension settings of the custom action to the manifest.json file. 

If a controller extension is already available for a page, you can copy the code and use it. 

Add the following configuration to the manifest.json file: 

"controlConfiguration": {
                "@com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1.LineItem": {
                  "tableSettings": {
                    "type": "ResponsiveTable"
                  },
                  "actions": {
                    "controller1": {
                      "press": "project1.ext.controller.controller1.exportSelectedRowData",
                      "visible": true,
                      "enabled": true,
                      "requiresSelection": true,
                      "text": "Export Selected Rows of Data"
                    }
                  }
                }
              }

Step 2: Implement the Export Logic in the Controller Extension 

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/m/MessageToast",
    "sap/ui/export/Spreadsheet",
    "sap/ui/export/library"
], function(MessageToast, Spreadsheet, exportLibrary) {
    'use strict';
    const EdmType = exportLibrary.EdmType;
    return {
        /**
         * Generated event handler.
         *
         * @Param oContext the context of the page on which the event was fired. `undefined` for list report page.
         * @Param aSelectedContexts the selected contexts of the table rows.
         */
        exportSelectedRowData:function(oContext, aSelectedContexts) {
            // 1. Guard clause: Ensure there are selected rows
            if (!aSelectedContexts || aSelectedContexts.length === 0) {
                MessageToast.show("Please select at least one row to export.");
                return;
            }

            // 2. Extract the raw JSON data from the selected contexts
            const aSelectedData = aSelectedContexts.map(function(oRowContext) {
                return oRowContext.getObject();
            });

            // 3. Define the columns for the Excel file
            const aColumns = [
                {
                    label: 'Department ID',
                    property: 'ID',    
                    type: EdmType.String             
                },
                {
                    label: 'Department Name',
                    property: 'deptName',
                    type: EdmType.String
                },
                {
                    label: 'Location',
                    property: 'location',
                    type: EdmType.String
                }
            ];

            // 4. Configure the Spreadsheet settings
            const mSettings = {
                workbook: {
                    columns: aColumns,
                    context: {
                        sheetName: 'Selected Row Data Sheet'
                    }
                },
                dataSource: aSelectedData,
                fileName: 'Selected_Departments.xlsx',
                worker: false // Set to false because we are passing client-side JSON data directly
            };

            // 5. Generate and download the Excel file
            const oSheet = new Spreadsheet(mSettings);
            
            oSheet.build()
                .then(function() {
                    MessageToast.show("Exported selected rows successfully.");
                })
                .catch(function(sMessage) {
                    MessageToast.show("Export failed: " + sMessage);
                })
                .finally(function() {
                    // Always destroy the spreadsheet instance to prevent memory leaks
                    oSheet.destroy();
                });
        }
    };
});

Now let's understand how the export functionality works internally 

Step 1: Import the Required Libraries 

Import the required SAPUI5 libraries for Excel export. 

  • sap/ui/export/Spreadsheet – Used to create and generate Excel files.  
  • sap/ui/export/library – Provides export-related data types such as EdmType, which are used while defining the Excel columns.  

Step 2: User Selects Rows 

The user selects one or more rows from the List Report table and clicks the Export Selected Rows of Data button. This action triggers the exportSelectedRowData() function. 

Step 3: Validate the Selection 

The application checks whether at least one row has been selected. If no rows are selected, a message is displayed to the user, and the export process is stopped. 

Step 4: Convert Context Objects to JSON 

The selected rows are received as binding context objects. Using the getObject() method, each context is converted into a plain JavaScript object. These objects are then stored in an array, which serves as the data source for the Excel export. 

Step 5: Define the Excel Columns 

The application defines the structure of the Excel file by specifying the column headers (label), the corresponding data properties (property), and the data type (EdmType). 

Step 6: Configure the Spreadsheet 

A Spreadsheet configuration object is created by specifying the workbook details, sheet name, column definitions, data source, and output file name. These settings determine how the Excel file will be generated. 

Step 7: Generate the Excel File 

A new Spreadsheet instance is created using the configuration object. The build() method then generates the Excel workbook and automatically downloads it to the user's system. 

Step 8: Handle Success or Errors 

The application displays a success message if the export is completed successfully. If an error occurs during the export process, an appropriate error message is displayed. 

Step 9: Destroy the Spreadsheet Object 

Finally, the Spreadsheet object is destroyed to release the resources occupied by it and prevent memory leaks. 

Output 

Selecting the Required Rows 

The user selects one or more rows from the List Report and clicks the Export Selected Rows of Data button. 

Excel Export in Progress 

After clicking the export button, the Spreadsheet API generates the Excel document. Once the process is completed, a success message is displayed. 

 Downloaded Excel File 

The generated Excel file is automatically downloaded to the user's local system with the configured file name (Selected_Departments.xlsx). 

Conclusion 

In this blog, we learned how to implement a custom action in SAP Fiori Elements that exports only the selected rows from the List Report into an Excel file. 

Unlike the standard Export functionality, which exports all records, this approach gives users greater flexibility by allowing them to download only the data they need. 

Since the implementation uses the sap.ui.export.Spreadsheet API, it can be easily reused across different SAP Fiori Elements applications with minimal changes to the column configuration. 

Thank you for reading! I hope you found this blog helpful and that it helps you implement a similar requirement in your own projects.

Happy Coding!

entry.title: reCAP 2026 – Recordings of our Annual CAP Developer Conference
entry.content:
On 15 July 2026, the CAP community came together for reCAP 2026 in St. Leon-Rot. Again, tickets were sold out within just a few minutes. We had more than 400 participants onsite and many more joining online.

For everyone who couldn’t attend live, most sessions were recorded. Below is a summary of the recorded sessions, along with links to the recordings. You can find all recordings on YouTube.


 

Opening & Keynote

Intro by Ole Lilienthal
Setting the stage, Ole gave an overview of the Autonomous Enterprise and how CAP plays a key role in enabling the SAP Business AI platform as the underlying platform for the Autonomous Enterprise. He also explained the importance of CAP in the age of AI.  

Recording link

Keynote by Daniel Hutzel
In his keynote, Daniel shared an overview of the biggest CAP achievements since the last reCAP, including AI-related topics.

Recording link

 

Main Track Sessions in Audimax

CAP Tools – What’s New and Hot by Christian Georgi
A tour through the latest and greatest in CAP tooling, including:

  • `cds repl` with queries
  • Debugging apps on Kyma
  • CDS Project outline in Visual Studio Code
  • CAP in browser

Recording link

Optimizing CAP Performance with Data Replication and Caching – Part II by Mike Zaschka
As a follow-up to last year’s talk on cds-caching, this session broadened the view to the full landscape of caching, replication, and federation strategies in the CAP ecosystem. Mike walked through a curated selection of solutions and offered practical guidance on which approach fits which scenario.

Recording link

AI-Assisted Development of CAP Apps by Daniel Schlachter and Stefan Rudi
A look at the CAP MCP development server, which gives AI coding assistants direct access to CAP documentation improving code quality in tools like Claude Code and GitHub Copilot.

Recording link

Expressions and Abstractions – Two Developer Superpowers in CAP by Patrice Bender and DJ Adams
A demonstration of two powerful CAP affordances: expressions (what they are, how to use them, and why they matter) and mocking (letting you iterate fast on data, auth, messaging, and remote services).

Recording link

CAP Plugins: Latest and Greatest by Max Eckert, Dries Van Vaerenbergh, Matthis Vansteenhuyse, and Lisa Julia Nebel
CAP plugins dramatically simplify integration with SAP BTP services. This session demystified the plugin model and spotlighted the enhanced attachments plugin, the renovated change tracking plugin, a new notifications plugin for Java, and the new SAP Build Process Automation plugin.

Recording link

Plug, Connect, Play: CAP Integration Made Effortless by Ram Prasad GS and Bilal El-Massoudi
An introduction to cap-connect, a production-ready, open-source CAP plugin from Aarini Consulting that brings first-class connection management to any CAP application. The talk also showcased how cap-connect powers Prodsphere, Aarini’s own CAP-based product.

Recording link

Production GenAI in CAP: Beyond the Demo by Moudhaffer Azizi
This session explored the architecture behind a production-grade CAP application on SAP BTP that uses RAG to analyse custom ABAP code against Clean Core standards. The session highlighted the journey from a single prompt to a resilient solution orchestrating 14 AI-powered operations.

Recording link

Beyond Vibe Coding: Engineering Deterministic CAP Applications with SDD by Maximilian Hartig and Abdulbasıt Gülşen
A live "0-to-1" walkthrough of Spec-Driven Development using the open-source Spec Kit in Visual Studio Code to transform a raw business requirement into a CAP application.

Recording link

From Click-Ops to Code-Ops (Terraform) by Nicholas Arefta
Nicholas demonstrated how to use Terraform to deploy multitenant apps on SAP BTP. He demonstrated provisioning of a Provider subaccount, deploying a multi-tenant application and onboarding a Subscriber completely hands-free.

Recording link

Custom Business Logic with Predefined Extension Points – Using CDS-Oyster by Nick Josipovic and Konrad Koschel
Together with Subscription Billing and Cloud Foundation, Nick and Konrad built an extensive Extension Box using CAP and the CDS-Oyster Code Sandbox enabling secure code extensions in a complex cloud environment.

Recording link

Implementing Runtime Plugins in CAP Java by Joshua Mitchell
Joshua demonstrated in his live session how to program a basic soft delete plugin for CAP Java using annotations, the IntelliJ debugger, and an expression evaluator.

Recording link

 

Side Track Sessions in W1/W2

Building Code-Based AI Agents with CAP and UI5: From Concept to Production by Wouter Lemaire

Using the Smart Monitoring Agent (previously demonstrated at SAP TechEd) as a real-world example, Wouter showed how to build intelligent, code-based AI agents by combining SAP AI Core with CAP and UI5. The session covered the complete journey from development to deployment.

Recording link

Building AI-enabled Applications with CAP by Max Eckert, Simon Engel, Markus Riedinger, and Peter W. Szabo

An overview of how CAP is evolving to meet the AI era including: exposing CAP services as endpoints for AI agents, leveraging the CAP AI plugin, exploring additional features, and building intelligent, code-driven agents.

Recording link

Building a CAP Application with Latest Best Practices and AI by Lukas Theis, Johannes Vogt, Marc Becker, and Robin de Silva Jayasinghe

A practical, end-to-end walkthrough from idea to production-ready CAP app, combining proven CAP architecture patterns with AI-assisted workflows.

Recording link

Declarative Programming Patterns in CAP - Less Code, More Power by Adrian Görler, Robin de Silva Jayasinghe, and Vitaly Kozyura

A look at how declarative mechanisms in CAP such as calculated elements, annotation-based constraints (e.g. @assert, @assert.range, @assert.format), and field control help to reduce custom code. The session also covered how status transition flows help reducing the need for custom validation logic while ensuring data integrity.

Recording link

Update from the cap-js-community - Experience the latest additions to CAP by Oliver Klemenz

A focused technical update on the latest enhancements in the cap-js-community, with a deep dive into the WebSocket modules and the newly introduced common utilities module.

Recording link

AI-Assisted Security Hardening of CAP Applications by Matthias Braun

Matthias showed how AI-powered tools can help identify authorization gaps, generate comprehensive security tests, and detect attack vectors. This significantly reduces the effort required to build and maintain secure CAP applications.

Recording link

Event Queues Scheduling by Sebastian Van Syckel, Dietrich Mostowoj, Thomas Bonk, and Lars Plessing
A deep dive into Transactional Event Queues, which allow scheduling of events and background tasks for asynchronous, exactly-once processing with strong resilience.

Recording link

Automated Testing with HANA Cloud by Armin Hatting
Armin presented practical approaches to integrating database-supported testing in CAP applications, setting up realistic test environments, and ensuring the functionality of SAP HANA Cloud native logic.

Recording link

Did you know about infix filters and projection functions? by Nico Schoenteich

A live-coding overview of infix filters and projection functions (Node.js) and how they were applied in a recent project to reduce custom service.

Recording link

 

Thank You

A huge thank-you to all speakers, attendees, and community members who made reCAP 2026 such an inspiring day. 🙌 The energy, the ideas, and the conversations are exactly what makes the CAP community special.

If you missed any session, use the recording links above to catch up.

We look forward to seeing how you use what you learned at reCAP 2026. If you have any feedback, please let us know.

Happy coding! 😊
